principles.paraphrase_en: A court may rectify a will under the relevant ordinance if it fails to accurately carry out the testator's intentions due to a clerical error or a misunderstanding of the testator's instructions.

principles.principle_label: Burden to set aside rectification order
principles.paraphrase_en: A party seeking to set aside a rectification order must demonstrate substantive grounds for doing so; the court will not grant such an application based on unsubstantiated complaints.

principles.principle_label: Submission to jurisdiction dispenses with service for contempt
principles.paraphrase_en: When a party has submitted to the jurisdiction of the court in the main action, personal service of subsequent contempt proceedings arising from that action is not required, even if the party is outside the jurisdiction.

principles.principle_label: Contempt as incidental to court's jurisdiction
principles.paraphrase_en: Once a court has established jurisdiction over a person in a claim, that jurisdiction extends to matters incidental to the claim, including enforcement proceedings such as contempt of court.

principles.principle_label: Discretion to dispense with service in contempt
principles.paraphrase_en: The court has a discretion to dispense with the requirement to serve an originating summons for contempt proceedings.
cited_statutes: Order 52, rule 3(4) of the Rules of the High Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Contempt proceedings should only be invoked as a measure of last resort.
An order cannot be enforced by committal for contempt unless its terms are expressed with sufficient clarity.

relationships.description: The judgment references this English authority in its review of principles regarding service of contempt proceedings.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the approach adopted by Deputy High Court Judge M K Liu in this case.

relationships.target_case_name: Crest Home Plc v Marks
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that jurisdiction includes matters incidental to the claim, such as enforcement.

relationships.target_case_name: Deutsche Bank AG v Sebastian Hold
relationships.description: The judgment cites this English authority in its discussion of principles related to service and jurisdiction in contempt proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: Mansour v Mansour
relationships.description: The judgment cites this English Court of Appeal case regarding the court's discretion to dispense with service in contempt proceedings.

principles.principle_label: Good reason for writ renewal - innocent mistake
principles.paraphrase_en: A failure to properly serve a writ within the required time period, if it results from an innocent mistake, can constitute a 'good reason' justifying the court's discretion to renew the writ's validity.
Neglect by a solicitor or their managing clerk, leading to a failure to serve a writ within time, can be a valid basis for a court to exercise its discretion to extend the writ's validity.

principles.principle_label: Mistaken service after expiry as good reason
principles.paraphrase_en: A mistaken belief that a writ has been properly served, when in fact its validity had expired, can constitute a 'good reason' for renewal.
The principle that an innocent mistake constitutes a good reason for renewing a writ applies even where the relevant limitation period for the action has expired.

principles.principle_label: Proper service on company registered address
principles.paraphrase_en: Serving a writ on a limited company's registered office is a normal and acceptable form of service, and is not improper even if the company's solicitors have requested future correspondence be sent to them, unless those solicitors specifically state they have authority to accept service.

relationships.target_case_name: Amadeaus
relationships.description: The judgment follows the approach in Amadeaus for determining what constitutes a 'good reason' for renewing a writ.

relationships.target_case_name: Holman v George Elliot and Company Limited
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Holman that solicitor's neglect can justify the renewal of a writ.

relationships.target_case_name: Jones v Jones
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Jones v Jones regarding mistaken service after expiry as a good reason.

relationships.target_case_name: Tan Kah Eng v Tan Eng Khiam
relationships.description: The judgment applies the holding from Tan Kah Eng that a mistaken belief about proper service can be a good reason.

relationships.target_case_name: Tan Kah Eng v Tan Eng Khiam (Court of Appeal)
relationships.description: The judgment follows the Court of Appeal's approval in Tan Kah Eng that the principles apply even after the limitation period expires.

candidates.case_name: SHINE ARCHIT. DESIGN LTD v. YEE WAI FONG AND OTHERS

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining whether a good reason has been shown for failing to serve a writ within the required time, it is relevant to consider if the non-service resulted from the plaintiff's deliberate choice. If it was a choice, there must be a good reason justifying that deliberate non-compliance with the time limit.

On an application to set aside an ex parte extension of a writ's validity, the court may consider not only the evidence presented at the time of the extension application but also evidence that subsequently comes to light.

It is an error of law to use evidence of events occurring after the date of an application to extend a writ's validity to reassess and nullify a 'good reason' that was prima facie established at the time of the application. This misapplies the principle allowing consideration of subsequent evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: Ganatra
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Ganatra that deliberate non-service requires a good reason for the choice.

relationships.target_case_name: China Medical Technologies Inc (in Liq) v Bank of China (Hong Kong) Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites Fok PJ's holding in China Medical Technologies regarding the consideration of evidence on applications concerning writ extensions.

relationships.target_case_name: National Commercial Bank v Haque
relationships.description: The judgment references the English Court of Appeal's decision in Haque as the authority for the principle permitting consideration of subsequent evidence.

judges: Before: Hon Linda Chan J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: For service of a writ under Order 10 to be valid, the defendant must be within the jurisdiction at the time the writ is served, not merely at the time it is placed in a letterbox.

principles.principle_label: Proof of subsequent knowledge and presence
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff may prove valid service by showing that the defendant subsequently came within the jurisdiction and gained knowledge of the writ, thereby curing an initial irregularity.

principles.principle_label: Invalidity of service without required affidavit
principles.paraphrase_en: Service of a writ is invalid if the plaintiff fails to file the affidavit required by Order 10 rule 1(3)(b)(i) when the writ could not have come to the defendant's knowledge within seven days.
A defendant's actual knowledge of proceedings alone does not validate service that was irregular because the defendant was outside the jurisdiction at the time of service.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of 'unless the contrary is shown'
principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing service validity, the court must consider the phrase 'unless the contrary is shown' in Order 10 rule 1(3), which relates to the date of service, not the defendant's location.

relationships.target_case_name: Barclays Bank of Swaziland Ltd v Hahn
relationships.description: The present judgment applies and follows the ratio from this House of Lords case that the defendant must be within the jurisdiction at the time of service.

relationships.target_case_name: Deng Minghui v Chau Shuk Ling
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Appeal's holding from this case regarding the invalidity of service when the defendant is outside the jurisdiction.

relationships.target_case_name: Luen Tat Watch Band Manufacturer Ltd v Li Shu Chung
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example where the court applied the ratio from Deng Minghui to find service irregular.

relationships.target_case_name: Emperor Prestige Credit Limited v King Pak Fu
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case where service was held invalid because the defendant was not in Hong Kong when the writ was placed in the letterbox.

relationships.target_case_name: Penrose Industries Ltd v Tam Yan Lung
relationships.description: The judgment notes this case was held to be wrongly decided for allowing actual knowledge alone to validate irregular service.

relationships.target_case_name: Wing Lung Bank Ltd v Ho Man Iam
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case which applied the principle from Barclays Bank regarding the defendant's presence at the time of service.

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant who has actual knowledge of legal proceedings cannot later challenge the validity or effectiveness of the service of those proceedings, regardless of where they were physically located at the time of service.

relationships.target_case_name: Deng Minghui v Chau Shuk Ling
relationships.description: The present judgment cites and relies upon the Court of Appeal's holding in this case regarding the effect of a defendant's actual knowledge of proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: Penrose Industries Ltd v Tam Yan Lung
relationships.description: The present judgment, through the cited authority, states that the earlier decision by Yeung J on the effect of a defendant's knowledge was wrongly decided.

principles.paraphrase_en: Courts must decide cases based on legal principles rather than sympathy alone, determining whether a reason is valid through the administration of justice.
The validity of a writ is not merely a procedural formality but involves substantive legal considerations where finality in litigation should be protected.
A party cannot be fixed with constructive knowledge of a Gazette Notice regarding company deregistration without legal authority establishing such a duty.
Costs should generally follow the event, meaning the unsuccessful party bears the costs of the successful party.

relationships.target_case_name: Chow Ching Man v Sun Wah Ornament Manufactory Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle established in Chow Ching Man that cases cannot be decided on sympathy alone but must be determined according to law.

relationships.target_case_name: Sealegend Holdings Ltd v China Taiping Insurance (HK) Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Sealegend Holdings that writ validity involves substantive considerations and finality in litigation should be protected.

principles.principle_label: Overruling of Penrose principle on service knowledge
principles.paraphrase_en: The principle established in Penrose Industries Ltd v Tam Yan Lung—that a defendant who acquires actual knowledge of proceedings cannot challenge the regularity or effectiveness of service—has been overruled by the Court of Appeal.

For service to be valid under Order 10, rule 1(2) of the Rules of the High Court, the plaintiff must demonstrate that the defendant was residing at the address at the time of service, and mere temporary absence does not invalidate service if the defendant ordinarily resides there.

principles.principle_label: Distinction between service in Hong Kong and outside jurisdiction
principles.paraphrase_en: The requirements for substituted service differ depending on whether the defendant is believed to be residing in Hong Kong or outside the jurisdiction; prior unsuccessful attempts at service under Order 11, rule 5A are necessary only when the defendant is outside Hong Kong.

relationships.target_case_name: Penrose Industries Ltd v Tam Yan Lung
relationships.description: The present judgment notes that the Court of Appeal has overruled the principle from Penrose that actual knowledge of proceedings validates irregular service.

relationships.target_case_name: Deutsche Bank AG, HK Branch v Zhang Hong Li
relationships.description: The judgment cites Deutsche Bank AG for principles regarding substituted service and the distinction between defendants residing in Hong Kong and those outside the jurisdiction.

principles.paraphrase_en: The Eshelby rule, derived from English law, addresses the amendment of pleadings to add claims for sums that became due after the writ was issued, where the original claim was for an instalment due before the writ.

principles.principle_label: Hong Kong approach to amendments curing defects
principles.paraphrase_en: Hong Kong law, following Wing Siu, does not permit an amendment to cure a defective claim by adding a necessary factual averment that could not have been pleaded as at the date of the writ.

The court's summary power to strike out a pleading should only be exercised in plain and obvious cases; where the legal viability of a cause of action is sensitive to the facts, a strike-out order is inappropriate.

relationships.target_case_name: Eshelby v Federated European Bank Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as the origin of the 'Eshelby rule' concerning amendments to pleadings.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Hin-Pro International Logistics Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Court of Appeal case for its commentary on the application of the Eshelby rule in winding-up petitions.

relationships.target_case_name: Wing Siu
relationships.description: The judgment states that Hong Kong law has followed the approach in Wing Siu regarding the impermissibility of certain amendments.

relationships.target_case_name: Lead Mile Ltd v Sino Peak Finance Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that an amendment cannot cure a defect by adding a factual averment unavailable at the writ's date.

relationships.target_case_name: Telecommunications & Technology Asia (BVI) Ltd v Outblaze Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this unreported case regarding the limits on amendments to cure defective claims.

principles.principle_label: Test for service by registered post
principles.paraphrase_en: Service of a writ by registered post is effective only when the defendant has actual notice of the proceedings, not merely upon delivery to the defendant's last known address.

A court is not bound to follow a prior appellate decision if it is inconsistent with a substantial line of subsequent first-instance decisions and has been weakened by inconsistency with higher authority.

relationships.description: The present judgment declines to follow Honour Finance, finding its authority weakened by inconsistency with other authorities.

relationships.target_case_name: Sinokawa Investment (Holdings) Ltd v Li Chun

relationships.target_case_name: Cosec Nominees Ltd v Lau Hon Ming

relationships.target_case_name: Phillip Securities (HK) Ltd v Lam Chi Bin Stanley
relationships.description: The judgment applies the analysis and principle from this case to support the 'actual notice' test.

relationships.target_case_name: Barclays Bank of Swaziland v Hahn
relationships.description: The judgment cites this House of Lords decision to highlight the inconsistency weakening the authority of Honour Finance.

principles.principle_label: Filing opposition does not confer party status
principles.paraphrase_en: Merely filing grounds of opposition to an injunction application does not automatically grant a person the status of a formal party to the proceedings or the right to make oral submissions at the hearing.

principles.principle_label: Service order does not create joinder
principles.paraphrase_en: A substituted service order that allows a person to be served with documents and to file grounds of opposition does not, by its terms, constitute that person as a formal party to the action. Party status requires a proper joinder application.
Where an application for permission to appeal is found to be totally without merit, the court may order that no party may request the matter be reconsidered at an oral inter partes hearing.

principles.principle_label: Retrospective leave to serve out of jurisdiction
principles.paraphrase_en: The court possesses the power to grant retrospective permission for service of legal documents outside the jurisdiction.

principles.principle_label: Test for retrospective leave to serve out
principles.paraphrase_en: The correct legal test for exercising the discretion to grant retrospective leave is whether there is a 'good reason' or 'good cause', not the stricter test of 'exceptional circumstances'.

principles.principle_label: Fresh writ issuance after extension refusal
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff may issue a new writ against the same defendant after being refused an extension of validity for the original writ, provided no limitation period issues arise.

principles.principle_label: Lis pendens exclusion for share sale agreements
principles.paraphrase_en: Legal proceedings concerning an agreement for the sale and purchase of company shares do not constitute a lis pendens under the Land Registration Ordinance, even if the company owns land, because such proceedings do not directly relate to land or an interest in land.

relationships.target_case_name: Smart Edge Ltd v. HG Property Investment HK Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment follows and applies the legal position established in the earlier judgment in the same case at specified paragraphs.

principles.paraphrase_en: For an action to be registrable as a pending land action (lis pendens), it must claim a proprietary right in the land itself, not merely seek to restrain the owner from disposing of the property.

principles.principle_label: Lien over shares vs. proprietary interest in land
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim to a lien over the shares of a company does not constitute a claim to a proprietary interest in the company's land.

principles.principle_label: Injunction does not create proprietary interest
principles.paraphrase_en: An injunction sought to enforce a lien (which is not itself a proprietary interest in land) does not transform that lien into a proprietary interest in land for the purposes of constituting a lis pendens.

relationships.target_case_name: Fung Kan Wai & anor v Leung Shui Fat & ors
relationships.description: The judgment references this case for the principle defining a registrable pending land action and discusses its application.

relationships.target_case_name: Calgary and Edmonton Land Co Ltd v Dobinson
relationships.description: The judgment cites this English authority, as referenced in Fung Kan Wai, for the classic definition of a registrable pending land action.

relationships.target_case_name: LY Group Development Ltd v East Canton Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the holding from this case that a lien over company shares is not a proprietary interest in the company's land.

principles.paraphrase_en: The court will not grant leave to serve proceedings out of jurisdiction unless the plaintiff satisfies it that Hong Kong is clearly and distinctly the more appropriate forum than the alternative, being the forum with the most real and substantial connection to the action.

principles.principle_label: Good arguable case requirement for service out
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff seeking leave to serve proceedings out of jurisdiction must demonstrate a good arguable case on the merits of its claim.
A foreign judgment on the merits can found a defence of res judicata in Hong Kong proceedings, precluding re-litigation of the same issues.
Applying to strike out proceedings for lack of jurisdiction does not, in itself, constitute a submission to the jurisdiction of the court.

Instituting proceedings in Hong Kong on matters that have already been litigated and decided against the plaintiff in a foreign court can constitute an abuse of process and cause hardship to the defendants.

relationships.target_case_name: Rambas Marketing Co LLC v Chow Kam Fai David
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Rambas that 'appropriate forum' means the forum with the most real and substantial connection to the action.

principles.principle_label: Valid service of writ requires presence in jurisdiction
principles.paraphrase_en: For service of a writ to be valid under Hong Kong rules, the defendant must be physically within the jurisdiction at the time of service. Service attempted when the defendant is outside the jurisdiction is invalid.

principles.principle_label: Forum non conveniens first hurdle - natural forum
principles.paraphrase_en: In a forum non conveniens application, the defendant must first establish that there is another available forum which is clearly more appropriate for the trial. If this first hurdle is not overcome, the court need not consider the comparative appropriateness of alternative forums.

principles.principle_label: Joinder of claims for interests of justice
principles.paraphrase_en: Where claims against multiple defendants are factually and legally inseparable, with the liability of some defendants dependent on the establishment of claims against a primary defendant, it is in the interests of justice for all claims to be determined together in a single proceeding.

When a witness is imprisoned overseas and may be extradited, video conferencing facilities may be the only practical method to secure their live testimony for trial, regardless of where the trial is held.

principles.paraphrase_en: A court cannot rely on documents or pleadings that have not been properly exhibited as evidence in the proceedings.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of unregistered instruments for locus standi
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining whether a person has standing to bring an application, the court may consider matters related to instruments that are pending registration.
If a transaction is found to be a sham, it is legally ineffective and cannot convey any interest in property.

principles.principle_label: Summary determination inappropriate with triable issue
principles.paraphrase_en: An originating summons should not be determined summarily if there is at least one triable issue that requires a full trial.

principles.principle_label: Material factual error as basis for appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: A material error of fact that forms a crucial basis for a judge's finding can constitute a valid ground for appeal.

principles.principle_label: Security for costs from court deposit
principles.paraphrase_en: Money paid into court as security for an application may be ordered to be held as security for the opposing party's costs of that application, unless the parties agree otherwise.

principles.paraphrase_en: Hong Kong courts routinely admit expert evidence on foreign law and do not consider it inherently unjust or problematic to resolve foreign law issues with such assistance.

principles.principle_label: Court's discretion to exclude witness testimony
principles.paraphrase_en: The court retains the discretion to refuse to hear a witness if it determines that their testimony would not be beneficial to the case.

principles.principle_label: Discretion on case transfer for investigation
principles.paraphrase_en: The trial court has the authority to evaluate a request to transfer a case for investigation and may refuse such a request if the existing case documents provide sufficient basis for the court to adjudicate without needing further investigation or witness hearings.

candidates.case_name: H, RS v. X, Y
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge S Lo in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: An injunction to freeze assets should not be granted if there are sufficient assets not at risk of dissipation that could satisfy the eventual financial award.
A court may grant an injunction to freeze assets if it finds, based on the evidence, a real risk that the assets will be dissipated, thereby jeopardizing the eventual award.

There is no invariable rule or default starting point that the costs of an interlocutory injunction application must be reserved to the trial or made 'costs in the cause'; the court should decide the proper costs order based on the merits of the application at the time it was heard.
Allegations of fraud are serious, and the court should investigate them, which may necessitate documentary discovery to verify the accounts given by the parties.

relationships.target_case_name: CYYC v TVKT
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from CYYC v TVKT that an injunction should not be granted if non-risk assets exist to satisfy a future award.

candidates.case_name: SOCAM DEVELOPMENT LTD (formerly known as SHUI ON CONSTRUCTION AND MATERIALS LTD) v. NEW PI (HONG KONG) INVESTMENT CO., LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge MK Liu in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A discovery order must precisely identify the documents or categories of documents required for disclosure. A failure to do so may result in a person making a false affidavit despite genuine compliance.

The relevance of documents for discovery is primarily determined by reference to the pleadings. However, a party cannot unilaterally dictate relevance by importing an issue into the pleadings; an opposing party can resist specific discovery on grounds of irrelevance even without a strike-out application.

principles.principle_label: Class of documents defined by nature
principles.paraphrase_en: A 'class of documents' for discovery purposes must be classified by its nature, not merely by grouping documents that go to a particular issue.
A party cannot simply identify an issue and request all documents relating to it. A class request for discovery must be adequately specific.

principles.principle_label: Prevention principle focuses on wrong, not intent
principles.paraphrase_en: To invoke the prevention principle (that no one shall take advantage of their own wrong), what matters is whether the act constitutes a wrong, not the motive or intent behind it.

relationships.target_case_name: Seabrook v British Transport Commission
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example where the description of documents for discovery was held to be a sufficient identification of a class.

candidates.case_name: ANGELA CHEN v. WAI WAI CHEN
judges: Before : Hon Barma JA, Au JA and Chow JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Appellate review of case management decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will only interfere with a judge's case management decision in rare circumstances where the appellant demonstrates the judge was clearly wrong, acted on an error of principle, made an irrational order, or created an injustice that prevents the trial court from performing its function.

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of fresh evidence on appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: New evidence is only admissible on appeal if it meets the established criteria set out in Ladd v Marshall, which typically requires that the evidence could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence for the original hearing, would probably have an important influence on the result, and is apparently credible.

A discovery application that was premature when made cannot be rendered timely merely by the passage of time; the correctness of the decision must be assessed based on the circumstances at the time of the application.

principles.principle_label: Reformulation of discovery requests on appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: A party should not generally be permitted to reformulate the classes of documents sought in discovery for the first time on appeal, particularly where no application to amend was made in the court below and no good reason is shown for allowing such reformulation.

principles.principle_label: Establishing prima facie existence of documents
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a prima facie case that documents exist for discovery purposes, more is required than mere speculation or expectations about what documents a professional should keep; positive evidence of existence is needed.

relationships.target_case_name: Ladd v Marshall
relationships.description: The judgment applies the established test from Ladd v Marshall regarding the admissibility of fresh evidence on appeal.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: NU PHARM LTD v. CHAMP GROUP LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Lok J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may vary a costs order nisi if the party seeking variation can demonstrate a sufficient reason, such as a material change in circumstances or a significant factor not previously considered, that justifies departing from the initial provisional order.
The general rule is that costs should follow the event, meaning the unsuccessful party is ordered to pay the costs of the successful party, unless there is a good reason to depart from this rule.

principles.principle_label: Apportionment of costs based on conduct
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may apportion costs, awarding only a fraction of the costs to a successful party, where that party's conduct during the litigation (such as pursuing unnecessary issues or failing to narrow the dispute efficiently) warrants a reduction from the full costs.

candidates.case_name: LYCV v. CYY
judges: Coram: HH Judge C.K. Chan in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: CHINA METAL RECYCLING (HOLDINGS) LTD (IN LIQUIDATION) v. DELOITTE TOUCHE TOHMATSU (A FIRM)
judges: Before: Hon Harris J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may approve the use of Technology Assisted Review (TAR) for electronic discovery, particularly when the parties agree to its use and the volume of documents is very large.

principles.principle_label: TAR as a supplement to manual review
principles.paraphrase_en: Technology Assisted Review is a tool to prioritize documents for manual review by legal professionals, not a replacement for human judgment in the discovery process.

relationships.target_case_name: Da Silva Moore v Publicis Groupe
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that computer-assisted review should be used where appropriate and is at least as accurate as manual review.

candidates.case_name: SARAS GOPI aka SARASWATHI GOPALAKRISHNAN v. BINA DEEGAN aka BINA CUNNINGHAM aka BINA GOPI
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Winnie Tsui in Chambers (Not Open to the Public)

principles.principle_label: Abuse of process - lack of genuine intention
principles.paraphrase_en: A court summons may be considered an abuse of process if, from its initiation to the present, there is clearly no genuine intention by the applicant to seriously pursue and argue the application.

A discovery application may be an abuse of process if it is not brought with a genuine desire to obtain discovery, but rather as a retaliatory measure in response to the other party's own discovery application.

candidates.case_name: CHOI HEI MAN KAMMIE v. METRO CITY MANAGEMENT LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Tony Ko in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: For pre-action discovery to be ordered, the requested documents must be directly relevant to issues likely to arise in the potential action.

principles.principle_label: Pre-action discovery necessity for saving costs
principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant for pre-action discovery must demonstrate that obtaining documents before commencing proceedings is necessary for saving costs, not merely convenient for avoiding minor pleading amendments later.
One objective of the pre-action discovery regime is to enable a potential plaintiff to assess whether they have a viable cause of action before initiating formal proceedings.

principles.principle_label: Oppression and prejudice in pre-action discovery
principles.paraphrase_en: Pre-action discovery is not oppressive or prejudicial merely because the documents would later be subject to automatic discovery if proceedings commence, especially when necessary for assessing claim strength.

A defendant in a pre-action discovery application will only be ordered to bear both parties' costs if their opposition was clearly unreasonable, their manner of opposition was unreasonable, or there was misconduct.

relationships.target_case_name: Zhang Shouen
relationships.description: The judgment references Zhang Shouen for the principle that pre-action discovery should not be ordered merely to avoid minor pleading amendments after ordinary discovery.

relationships.target_case_name: Lu Cheng Te Raymond
relationships.description: The judgment applies the observation from Lu Cheng Te Raymond regarding the purpose of pre-action discovery to enable assessment of claim viability.

relationships.target_case_name: Lau Tsz Hung Ringo v First Refrigeration Engineering Limited
relationships.description: The judgment follows the costs principles established in Lau Tsz Hung Ringo regarding when a defendant should bear both parties' costs in pre-action discovery applications.

candidates.case_name: SL v. CPYD
judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge J. Chow in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A court order for document disclosure must precisely identify the specific documents or clearly defined categories of documents required. Vague or overly broad descriptions risk placing the disclosing party in jeopardy of swearing a false affidavit despite acting honestly.

principles.principle_label: Peruvian Guano relevance for document classes
principles.paraphrase_en: When seeking a class of documents, every document within that class must satisfy the Peruvian Guano test of relevance. The court cannot order disclosure of an entire class if it includes documents that are not relevant, and this requirement cannot be bypassed by bundling relevant and irrelevant documents together.

A party seeking disclosure from a non-party must demonstrate why the specific documents are necessary for the fair disposal of the proceedings. Mere relevance or a desire to investigate is insufficient if the party has not pursued other available legal avenues regarding that non-party.

It can be oppressive to order a non-party to disclose documents if the applicant has been inactive in pursuing related claims against that non-party through other appropriate legal procedures, suggesting a lack of genuine necessity for the documents in the current proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: Tullett Prebon (Hong Kong) Limited
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from Tullett Prebon regarding the requirement that all documents in a sought class must be relevant under the Peruvian Guano test.

relationships.target_case_name: Three Rivers District Council v Bank of England (No 4)
relationships.description: The judgment cites the principle from Three Rivers (as adopted in Tullett Prebon) that a class of documents for discovery must not mix relevant and irrelevant documents.

candidates.case_name: GREEN SUMMIT HOLDING PTE. LTD. v. XUDI TRADING CO., LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Chambers (by paper disposal)

candidates.case_name: VICTORY CITY INTERNATIONAL HOLDINGS LTD (In Liquidation) AND ANOTHER v. DELOITTE TOUCHE TOHMATSU (a firm)
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Andrew Li in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Applications for discovery before a writ is issued or before a statement of claim is served are considered exceptional or unusual, and should not be granted unless in the most exceptional circumstances to prevent 'fishing' for a case.

In the absence of formal pleadings, the relevance of documents for a pre-action or pre-statement of claim discovery application can be determined by reference to the affidavit supporting the application, which must sufficiently articulate the intended claim.

A highly relevant factor in granting early discovery is the likely saving of costs resulting from the plaintiff being able to plead their case with greater particularity from the outset, avoiding the need for subsequent amendments.

principles.principle_label: Prima facie case threshold for discovery
principles.paraphrase_en: An application for pre-pleading discovery is not a 'fishing' expedition if the applicant clearly has a prima facie case against the respondent based on the matters identified in the supporting affidavit.

relationships.target_case_name: Lott v PSA Automobiles SA
relationships.description: The judgment cites this English case to support the proposition that pre-action disclosure is regarded as exceptional in nature.

relationships.target_case_name: RHM Foods v Bovril
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that pre-writ discovery should only be granted in the most exceptional circumstances to prevent 'fishing' for a case.

relationships.target_case_name: Bermuda International Securities
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from this case that cost savings from enabling more particular pleading is a highly relevant factor for granting early discovery.

relationships.target_case_name: Marshall
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that the likely saving of costs from avoiding amendments is a relevant factor in granting early discovery.

candidates.case_name: CHONG CHI KI PIERRE v. GLORY SKY PRECIOUS METALS LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: His Honour Judge H. Au-Yeung (Paper Disposal)

candidates.case_name: REALORD GROUP HOLDINGS LTD AND ANOTHER v. WIN DYNAMIC LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: An ancillary order for discovery in support of an injunction should not be made for the primary purpose of investigating whether a breach of the injunction has occurred.
The test for granting an ancillary order for discovery is whether it is just and convenient to do so to ensure the effectiveness of the injunction, not the seriousness of any prior breach.

Vague or non-detailed explanations from a party subject to an injunction do not, by themselves, constitute evidence or justify an inference that a breach of the injunction has occurred or that there is a real risk of breach.

principles.principle_label: Need for discovery to be ancillary
principles.paraphrase_en: For discovery to be ordered as ancillary to an injunction, the applicant must show the discovery is needed to make the injunction effective and is directly connected to the court's primary jurisdiction in granting the injunction.

relationships.target_case_name: RACP Pharmaceutical Holdings Ltd v Li Xiabo
relationships.description: The judgment discusses and appears to rely on the principle from this case that discovery should not be ordered to investigate whether a defendant has dissipated funds subject to the underlying claim.

candidates.case_name: LAI DOMINIC YUK TSUN v. HANWOOD ENTERPRISES LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge MK Liu in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: For the purpose of serving a document by leaving it at an office, it is sufficient if the document is left at the office premises; it does not need to be left inside the office itself. Leaving it immediately outside the office door can constitute valid service.
Where a witness's evidence is uncontradicted and there is no reason to doubt it, the court is entitled to accept that evidence as fact.

The court may draw an inference about the handling of a document based on evidence of the general office practice for dealing with items left outside, in the absence of specific recollection or contradictory evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: Ladd v Marshall
relationships.description: The judgment references the Ladd v Marshall criteria for admitting fresh evidence, specifically discussing whether its first criterion is satisfied.

relationships.target_case_name: 深圳巿均翔房地產經紀有限公司 v Hongkong Zhongxing Group Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that leaving a document at a registered office can be effected by leaving it outside the office door.

candidates.case_name: KH FOUNDATIONS LTD v. CHUN YIP CONSTRUCTION CO LTD
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The enforceability of a liquidated damages clause depends on its construction, requiring the court to assess its purpose, whether the party has a legitimate interest in ensuring performance, and whether the stipulated sum is a genuine pre-estimate of loss rather than extravagant or unconscionable.

candidates.case_name: TWF v. HWKR
judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge M. Lam in Chambers (Not open to public)

principles.principle_label: Discovery relevance test for third-party disputes
principles.paraphrase_en: In disputes over beneficial ownership involving third parties (not spouses), the legal approach must be the same as in ordinary civil litigation (like the Chancery Division), not the discretionary ancillary relief process between spouses.

principles.principle_label: Peruvian Guano test for document discovery
principles.paraphrase_en: A document is relevant for discovery if it relates to matters in question in the action, including documents that could provide evidence, advance one's own case, damage the opponent's case, or lead to a train of inquiry with those consequences.

principles.principle_label: Limits on discovery - no fishing or creating issues
principles.paraphrase_en: Discovery is not permitted to turn a non-issue into an issue, to allow fishing for improprieties, or to hunt through documents hoping to find a new case. Relevance is determined by reference to the pleadings.

principles.principle_label: Interrogatories relevance and prohibition on fishing
principles.paraphrase_en: Interrogatories must relate to matters in question between parties and be necessary for fair disposal or cost savings. They cannot be used as fishing expeditions to discover a case unknown to the party.

principles.principle_label: Property law applies to third-party ownership disputes
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining whether property is owned by a spouse or a third party, the court must apply ordinary property law principles, not family law discretionary principles.

relationships.target_case_name: TL v ML & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from TL v ML that third-party beneficial ownership disputes must be approached on the same legal basis as Chancery Division proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: Compagnie Financière du Pacifique v Peruvian Guano Company
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Peruvian Guano test for determining relevance of documents in discovery.

relationships.target_case_name: Melvin Waxman v Li Fei Yu & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle that discovery cannot be used to turn a non-issue into an issue.

relationships.target_case_name: Re the Estate of Ng Chan Wah
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle that discovery cannot be used to hunt for improprieties or pursue speculative inquiries.

relationships.target_case_name: Lee Nui Foon v Ocean Park Corp (No 2)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle that interrogatories cannot be used as fishing expeditions to discover unknown cases.

relationships.target_case_name: Bhura v Bhura & Ors (No.2)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle that property law must be used to determine ownership in third-party disputes.

candidates.case_name: CKP v. SYK
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge G. Own in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The obligation of full and frank disclosure in civil proceedings requires parties to reveal all documents and information in their possession that are relevant to the matters in issue, even if such documents advance the opponent's case or damage their own case.

principles.principle_label: Determining relevance in family ancillary relief
principles.paraphrase_en: In family ancillary relief cases where no formal pleadings exist, the court may determine the relevance of documents or information by referring to the parties' affirmations, their Form E asset disclosures, and answers provided to questionnaires.

A court order for discovery must identify with precision the specific documents or categories of documents to be disclosed, to avoid placing the person giving discovery in jeopardy of swearing a false affidavit despite acting honestly.

When discovery is sought for a class of documents, every document within that class must individually satisfy the Peruvian Guano relevance test. The court cannot order disclosure of an entire class if it includes documents that are not relevant.

principles.principle_label: Burden of showing relevance for third-party discovery
principles.paraphrase_en: A party applying for discovery against a non-party (e.g., via a Banker's Summons) bears the burden of demonstrating the relevance of the documents sought to the issues in the ongoing proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: Compagnie Financiere du Pacifique v Peruvian Guano Company
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Peruvian Guano principle to define the scope of full and frank disclosure and the test for relevance in discovery.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Estate of Ng Chan Wah
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that relevance is to be determined by reference to pleadings, adapting it for the family law context.

relationships.target_case_name: Three Rivers District Council v Bank of England (No.4)
relationships.description: The judgment cites Chadwick LJ's reasoning from this case, via Tullen Prebon, regarding the requirement for each document in a class to be relevant.

relationships.target_case_name: Tullen Prebon (Hong Kong) Limited
relationships.description: The judgment follows the reasoning of To J in this case, which adopted the principles from Three Rivers on class document disclosure.

relationships.target_case_name: CLS v LPKP
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as useful and instructive on the relevant legal principles.

candidates.case_name: KONCI (1989) LTD v. CHOR JUN WO DAVID formerly known as CHOR KI KWONG DAVID
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Sara Tong SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: At the interlocutory stage, the sworn affidavit of a party regarding discovery is considered final and binding. This can only be disregarded if the court is satisfied, based not on conflicting affidavits but on the produced documents, the content of the party's own affidavit, admissions in pleadings, or the inherent circumstances of the case, that the affidavit does not accurately state what it should.

principles.principle_label: Standard for challenging discovery affidavit under O.24 r.16
principles.paraphrase_en: The principle that a discovery affidavit is conclusive does not apply in a more stringent or different manner for applications made under Order 24, rule 16 of the Rules of the High Court (for failure to comply with discovery orders). The standard for challenging the affidavit remains the same.
The conclusiveness of a discovery affidavit can be overcome if the applicant can demonstrate, through evidence filed by the other party or documents exhibited by them, that the affidavit is untrue.

When assessing whether a party must currently possess documents, the court will consider the significant lapse of time since the documents were created. A long passage of time is a relevant factor in determining whether the conclusiveness of a discovery affidavit should be displaced.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Kar Gee Mimi v Severn Villa Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that a discovery affidavit is conclusive at the interlocutory stage, subject to specific qualifications.

relationships.target_case_name: Ka Wah Bank Ltd v Low Chung-song
relationships.description: The judgment finds that this case does not support the proposition that the conclusiveness principle applies more stringently under Order 24 rule 16, as it was decided on the basis of a lack of conscientious effort and patently inadequate discovery.

relationships.target_case_name: Tsoy Kai v Wu John Baptist
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from this case that the conclusiveness principle has qualifications, allowing a challenge if the affidavit can be shown to be a lie based on other evidence or documents from the same party.

candidates.case_name: KONEW CAPITAL INTERNATIONAL LTD v. WONG KONG HEUNG formerly known as WONG KWONG HEUNG CANDY
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Damian Wong in Chambers (Open to public)

candidates.case_name: HONG KONG RESORT CO LTD v. TOWN PLANNING BOARD
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Barma JA and G Lam JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For a consideration to be relevant to a planning authority's decision, it must be of a planning nature, which generally includes matters relating to land use and development. However, whether a specific consideration is material depends on the particular circumstances of the case.

Planning decisions should consider the impact on the broader locality, not just the individual site. It is proper to assess the side effects or consequences that granting permission for one site may have on other sites within the same area.

A planning authority may consider whether approving an application would set an undesirable precedent for similar applications, but this requires a proper comparison between the site in question and other allegedly similar sites.
The distinction between granting planning permission and the implementation of that permission is a relevant principle in planning law, particularly in applications to amend an Outline Zoning Plan.

A planning authority has a duty to make sufficient inquiries to properly inform itself before making a decision, particularly to ascertain whether an application is consistent with the planning intention for the area.

relationships.target_case_name: Royal Billion Investment Ltd v The Town Planning Board
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that implementation is a relevant consideration in section 12A applications to amend an OZP.

relationships.target_case_name: Collis Radio Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes this case on the basis that it arose under a different statutory regime (the UK's Town and Country Planning Act 1971).

candidates.case_name: SUCCESS LANE DEVELOPMENT LTD v. FERGURSON HONG KONG LTD t/a NEW WORLD MILLENNIUM HONG KONG HOTEL
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Gary C C Lam in Chambers (Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: The trial court must make its best effort to find facts based on the evidence presented, even when the evidence is not ideal. The existence of imperfect evidence does not automatically make expert evidence impracticable or meaningless, as expert input provides a better evidential basis for fact-finding.

If expert witnesses find a provided list of items insufficient for their assessment, they should state this in their expert reports. If necessary, further applications can be made under 'liberty to apply' or other appropriate procedural mechanisms to implement the court's order.

relationships.target_case_name: Patrick Hung Hing Yu v Ho Yin Man
relationships.description: The judgment considers but does not find support for the plaintiff's proposition in this case.

relationships.target_case_name: Beijing Tong Gang Da Sheng Trade Co Ltd v Allen & Overy
relationships.description: The judgment considers but does not find support for the plaintiff's proposition in this case.

relationships.target_case_name: T v C
relationships.description: The judgment considers but does not find support for the plaintiff's proposition in this case.

candidates.case_name: CMBICDHAW INVESTMENTS LTD v. LI LEI AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A party's affidavit regarding discovery is considered conclusive on matters of relevance, unless the court can be satisfied from the documents produced, the affidavit's own contents, admissions in pleadings, or necessary circumstances that the affidavit does not accurately state what it should.

principles.principle_label: Discretion to refuse relief from sanctions
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's discretion to deny relief from sanctions for non-compliance with court orders is not restricted to cases of intentional and contumelious disregard. Negligence, incompetence, or indolence in failing to comply with one or multiple orders may, depending on the circumstances, constitute sufficient fault to justify refusing an extension of time or relief.

If a party believes the opposing party has not provided all discoverable documents, the proper course is to apply to the court for further discovery, rather than asserting that relief from sanctions should be denied on that basis.

Where there are disputed issues of fact on the pleadings, those matters are to be determined at trial and cannot be used at the discovery stage to render a party's affirmations incredible or their disclosure illusory.

relationships.target_case_name: GE Capital Corporate Finance Group Ltd v Bankers Trust Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that a party's discovery affidavit is conclusive on relevance.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Kar Gee Mimi v Severn Villa Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as further authority for the principle on the conclusiveness of a discovery affidavit.

relationships.target_case_name: Ka Wah Bank Limited v Low Chung-song and another
relationships.description: The judgment references this case in the context of a submission regarding the procedure when a party alleges inadequate discovery.

candidates.case_name: KITAHARA HONG KONG CO, LTD v. CHENG KAM WAH GRACE (鄭金華) (ALSO KNOWN AS CHENG GRACE OR 鄭詠騰)
judges: Before: Master Bianca Yu in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A court order for specific discovery must precisely identify the documents or categories of documents required for disclosure to avoid placing the disclosing party at risk of inadvertently swearing a false affidavit despite acting honestly.

When determining the quantum of security for costs, the court must consider whether the amount sought is excessive in light of the simplicity of the claim and defence, adopting a broad, holistic approach to decide what amount is just.

principles.principle_label: Costs order for security for costs application
principles.paraphrase_en: Even where a party succeeds in obtaining an order for security for costs, the court may order that party to bear the costs of the application if the quantum ultimately awarded is substantially lower than what was sought, and a different party's proposed quantum is accepted.

candidates.case_name: NEW WELL MASTER CO LTD (Formerly known as WELL MASTER CO LTD) v. TIDJAN ANANTO
judges: Before: Hon K Yeung J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Substitution of parties under O.15 r.7
principles.paraphrase_en: The court has discretion under Order 15 rule 7 of the Rules of the High Court to order that a person to whom a party's interest or liability has been assigned, transmitted, or devolved be made a party to the proceedings, if necessary to ensure effective and complete determination of all matters in dispute.
cited_statutes: Rules of the High Court O.15 r.7

principles.paraphrase_en: When making a representation order under Order 15 rule 15, the court must consider whether the proposed appointee is the appropriate person, which may involve examining challenges to their status or the merits of competing claims.
cited_statutes: Rules of the High Court O.15 r.15

relationships.target_case_name: Lily Cheung v The Official Solicitor
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the approach from Lily Cheung regarding the court's consideration of the appropriateness of a proposed appointee under a representation order.

candidates.case_name: CHUNG FUNG PING v. CITIBANK (HONG KONG) LTD
judges: Coram: Before Deputy District Judge Alfred Cheng in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A party seeking pre-action discovery must demonstrate they are disabled from commencing their claim without the documents sought; mere knowledge of the documents' existence is insufficient.
An application for pre-action discovery requires the applicant to first identify a viable cause of action against the respondent.

An investment recommendation is generally an opinion, not a statement of fact, and is not actionable as misrepresentation unless it is not honestly held or is based on an incorrect implicit factual basis.

principles.principle_label: Ability to plead claim without discovery
principles.paraphrase_en: Pre-action discovery is not warranted if the applicant can identify the alleged wrong (e.g., misrepresentation) and quantify their loss without the requested documents.
Costs follow the event, and substantial costs may be summarily assessed as reasonable where a respondent had to prepare significant evidence to oppose an extensive but unsuccessful application.

candidates.case_name: TYP v. CWY AND ANOTHER
judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Elaine Liu in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: When property is transferred without payment of consideration, and the transferee is aware that they are not the beneficial owner, a resulting trust arises in favor of the transferor or intended beneficiary.

The purpose of discovery in legal proceedings is not to permit a party to conduct a fishing expedition through voluminous documents in the hope of uncovering improprieties or grounds for further investigation.

relationships.target_case_name: In the estate of Ng Chan Wah, Deceased
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case regarding the improper use of discovery as a fishing expedition.

candidates.case_name: NPYJ v. SMRC
judges: Before : Hon Kwan VP and Cheung JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Appeal standard for case management decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal against a case management decision, such as a ruling on a discovery application, can only succeed on limited grounds. The appellant faces a very high hurdle and must demonstrate that the judge either erred in principle, made an irrational order, or reached a decision that was clearly wrong and would result in injustice or prevent the trial court from performing its function.

When determining an application for specific discovery, the court must consider whether the documents sought are relevant and necessary for the fair disposal of the matter or for saving costs. The court may also consider whether the requests constitute a disproportionate fishing exercise.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Kar Gee Mimi v Severn Villa Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts and applies the high appeal standard for case management decisions as articulated by Kwan JA in that case.

candidates.case_name: ZPMC OFFSHORE SERVICE CO LTD (Formerly known as SHANGHAI ZHENHUA HEAVY INDUSTRIES SHIPPING CO LTD) v. PHILIP JEFFREY ADKINS RBF HK LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kenneth Wong in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Functus officio regarding costs of struck-out claim
principles.paraphrase_en: A court that has struck out a claim is generally functus officio (having performed its office) and should not subsequently rule on the costs of that claim when dealing with a separate application to discontinue a counterclaim.

principles.principle_label: Anomaly of adjudicating costs of non-existent claim
principles.paraphrase_en: It is anomalous and inappropriate for a court to decide the costs of a claim that is no longer in existence when the substantive application before it relates to the discontinuance of a separate counterclaim.

principles.principle_label: No order as to costs upon discontinuance of counterclaim
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a counterclaim is discontinued, the appropriate costs order may be to make no order as to the costs of the counterclaim.

candidates.case_name: WONG SZE LING AND ANOTHER v. YOUNG CHEUNG LEE AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Linda Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Abuse of process in repeated applications
principles.paraphrase_en: A party cannot raise a point in a later application if it was available but not pursued in an earlier interlocutory application, unless there has been a material change in circumstances or new facts.

The principle preventing the raising of points in later applications applies not only where a court has already determined an earlier application, but also where there has been no prior determination by the court.

relationships.target_case_name: Chanel Ltd v FW Woolworth & Co Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts and applies the principle first established in Chanel regarding abuse of process when raising points in subsequent applications.

relationships.target_case_name: Gee
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from Gee that the principle is founded on the responsibility to take a point when first reasonably available.

relationships.target_case_name: Compania Sud Americana De Vapores SA
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the broad application of the Chanel principle beyond situations with prior court determinations.

candidates.case_name: WONG TSZ HANG by her uncle and next friend CHU MAN KONG v. INDUSTRIAL AND COMMERCIAL BANK OF CHINA (ASIA) LTD
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge Levy in Chambers (Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: The default position for costs arising from an amendment application is that they should be paid 'in any event' rather than 'forthwith', due to the practical difficulty of assessing future costs at the time of the amendment.

principles.principle_label: Burden for departing from usual costs order
principles.paraphrase_en: A party seeking a departure from the usual costs order (such as requesting costs be paid 'forthwith' instead of 'in any event') bears the burden of demonstrating unusual features or grounds justifying that departure.

candidates.case_name: CHINA MEDICAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC (In Liquidation) AND OTHERS v. THE BANK OF EAST ASIA, LTD
judges: Before: Hon Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Liability under section 275 of the Companies (Winding Up and Miscellaneous Provisions) Ordinance requires that the defendant themselves carried on the business of the company in a fraudulent manner; it is not equivalent to providing dishonest assistance to directors who are breaching their duties.

relationships.target_case_name: China Medical Technologies, Inc & Ors v Bank of China
relationships.description: The present judgment notes that a prior related decision (the BOC Decision) had already determined many of the same issues raised in the defendant's strike-out application.

candidates.case_name: SAM WOO BORE PILE FOUNDATION LTD v. NEW EDEX CONSTRUCTION LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Jonathan Wong in Chambers

candidates.case_name: AU DIN HAR ELLEN v. AU YEE WAN also known as YEE WAN AU AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The court must assess the facts and parties' conduct objectively, not based on their unproven subjective state of mind or misconceptions of law.

candidates.case_name: CHAN YAT FUNG v. YING SHING DESIGN & DECORATION LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Phoebe Man in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Striking out for non-compliance with winding-up rules
principles.paraphrase_en: A winding-up petition may be struck out if the petitioner fails to comply with procedural rules, such as obtaining the Registrar's Certificate of compliance without undue delay, especially where such non-compliance is part of a broader abuse of process.

principles.principle_label: Abuse of process in winding-up petitions
principles.paraphrase_en: Non-compliance with procedural rules and want of prosecution by the petitioner can together constitute an abuse of process, justifying the striking out of a winding-up petition.

Although Rule 29 of the Winding-Up Rules does not specify a timeframe, the Practice Direction indicates the Registrar's Certificate should be obtained without undue delay, and failure to obtain it within three months of the petition date without good reason may lead to dismissal.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Royal Mutual Benefit Building Society
relationships.description: The judgment cites the English case for the proposition that failure to comply with a rule equivalent to Rule 29 ordinarily leads to the petition being struck out.

candidates.case_name: CHAN WS AND ANOTHER v. CC Bank
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Yuen and Au JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An overstatement of the indebtedness in a statutory demand does not automatically entitle the debtor to have the demand set aside. The key question is whether injustice would be caused to the debtor by allowing the demand to stand.

The principle that an overstatement does not automatically invalidate a statutory demand applies both to applications to set aside the demand and to hearings considering whether to make a bankruptcy order.

A creditor who knows that a statutory demand overstates the debt may be unable to properly confirm under section 6A(1)(a) of the Bankruptcy Ordinance that the debtor has failed to satisfy the demand when presenting a bankruptcy petition.

When considering whether to set aside a statutory demand with an overstatement, the court must assess whether injustice would be caused to the debtor, considering factors such as whether the debtor would have been able or prepared to pay the correct amount.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Ip Pui Man Nina
relationships.description: The present judgment follows and applies the principle from Re Ip Pui Man Nina that an overstatement in a statutory demand does not automatically invalidate it if no injustice would be caused.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Kwok Chok Yee
relationships.description: The judgment follows the same principle established in Re Kwok Chok Yee regarding overstatements in statutory demands and the injustice test.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Debtor (No 1 of 1987)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the English Court of Appeal's reasoning that the overstatement principle specifically applies to applications to set aside statutory demands.

relationships.target_case_name: Winterthur Life Insurance Company v Au Oi Fong Susana
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported case as part of a line of authorities applying the overstatement principle.

relationships.target_case_name: Li Wo Hing v Raiffeisen Bank International AG (Beijing Branch)
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported decision as additional authority supporting the application of the overstatement principle.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Lo Kwai Ying Louisa
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported case as further authority in the line of cases applying the overstatement principle.

candidates.case_name: ASIAHI IWASA WA & ASSOCIATES MANAGEMENT CONSULTANTS LTD v. SHIBAKAWA (HONG KONG) LTD
judges: Before: Mr Recorder Pow, SC in Chambers

candidates.case_name: YOUNG CRYSTAL LTD AND OTHERS v. HANG SENG BANK LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Le Pichon in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's wide case management powers should not be exercised to effectively determine a substantive issue in the case before a scheduled hearing, especially when related appeals are pending.
A case management direction should not foreclose the determination of an important underlying substantive issue in the litigation.

candidates.case_name: XU QIANGLI AND ANOTHER v. ELABS COMPANY LTD
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge G. Chow in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A pleading of dishonest receipt or fraud is insufficient if the alleged facts are equally consistent with honest conduct; the court cannot infer dishonesty from facts that could also indicate innocence.

principles.principle_label: Insufficient pleading of proprietary interest retention
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim for a proprietary interest in funds requires a sufficiently pleaded case that the defendant retained the specific property in which the plaintiff claims an interest; merely pleading that funds were transferred between accounts is insufficient.

relationships.target_case_name: Peconic Industrial Development Ltd & anor v Yu Ka Hong Paul & anor
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Peconic that the court cannot infer dishonesty from facts equally consistent with honesty.

relationships.target_case_name: Three Rivers District Council & others
relationships.description: The judgment applies principles from Three Rivers regarding the standard for pleading dishonesty.

relationships.target_case_name: JSP International SRO
