principles.principle_label: Interim injunctions for internal company disputes
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may grant interlocutory injunctive relief in matters concerning the internal management of a company and compliance with its Articles of Association.

principles.principle_label: Scope of injunctive relief in corporate governance
principles.paraphrase_en: Interim relief can be sought to restrain individuals from holding out or acting as directors or officers of a company unless they are properly elected or appointed under its Articles of Association.
The court's role in such applications is to ensure compliance with the company's Articles of Association, particularly regarding the proper election and appointment of directors and officers.

candidates.case_name: WANFU FINANCE COMPANY LTD v. TSE TONG LAM ANTONIO AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge G. Chow in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: At the interlocutory injunction stage, the court's primary concern is the risk of making an incorrect decision before trial. The court will adopt the course that minimizes the potential injustice if its interim decision later proves wrong.

principles.principle_label: Serious question to be tried threshold
principles.paraphrase_en: The requirement to show a 'serious issue to be tried' is not a high threshold. The plaintiff must demonstrate prospects of success that are substantive and real, not merely speculative. The exact probability of success (whether high or low) is not decisive, unless the prospects are so negligible that they lack substance.

principles.principle_label: Land registration as evidence not title
principles.paraphrase_en: The land registration system under the relevant ordinance is one of deeds registration, not title registration. Its purpose is to facilitate the tracing of title, not to confer title itself. Registration provides prima facie evidence of ownership derived from the registered document but does not create rights or title, especially where external evidence shows the title does not exist.

Common guidelines for granting interlocutory injunctions include assessing the merits of the plaintiff's claim and the balance of convenience. These are linked to the fundamental principle of minimizing injustice, as injustice may arise if no serious question is shown or if a party would suffer substantial prejudice from the grant or refusal of the injunction.

relationships.target_case_name: King Fung Vacuum Ltd v Toto Toys Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment references this case in the context of a submission regarding delay in bringing an injunction application.

candidates.case_name: MTM LAB JAPAN LTD AND OTHERS v. ABACUS LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: His Honour Judge MK Liu in Chambers (Open to Public) (Remote Hearing)

principles.principle_label: Threshold for serious question to be tried
principles.paraphrase_en: For an interlocutory application, the plaintiff only needs to demonstrate that there is a serious question to be tried regarding the defendant's involvement in the alleged acts, which is not a very high threshold to meet.

principles.principle_label: No common law tort of harassment
principles.paraphrase_en: Hong Kong common law does not recognize a standalone tort of harassment.

relationships.target_case_name: Pong Seong Teresa & Ors v Chan Norman
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the proposition that there is no tort of harassment at common law in Hong Kong.

candidates.case_name: MING YANG CAPITAL LTD v. CHINA VERED ASSET MANAGEMENT (HONG KONG) LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Norman Nip SC in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Material facts for ex parte applications
principles.paraphrase_en: Material facts are those relevant to the court's weighing exercise in deciding whether to grant an order. The test is not whether disclosure would have changed the outcome, but whether the undisclosed facts were relevant and should have been considered in the balancing process.

relationships.target_case_name: Xie Li Xin v Law Ka Yan, Thompson & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the test for material non-disclosure in ex parte applications as stated in this case.

candidates.case_name: LU YONGDE v. ALLAN YAP AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Andrew Li in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Court's primary duty to ascertain truth
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's fundamental obligation is to determine the truth by considering the best available evidence, and no party can prevent another from accessing a witness of fact or compelling their testimony.

A witness of fact is not the property of any party; the court can compel any person with knowledge of relevant facts to give evidence, and parties cannot prohibit each other from contacting or subpoenaing such witnesses.

principles.principle_label: Injunction not granted if damages adequate
principles.paraphrase_en: An injunction should not be granted if an award of damages would provide an adequate remedy for the alleged wrong.

principles.principle_label: Delay and irreparable damage for injunction
principles.paraphrase_en: Unexplained and inordinate delay in applying for an injunction, coupled with a failure to demonstrate that irreparable damage would result if the injunction is not granted, are grounds for refusing the injunction.

principles.principle_label: Delay not a defence to enforcement
principles.paraphrase_en: Inordinate delay alone does not constitute a defence to an application to enforce the terms of an agreement; to resist enforcement, a defendant must show the applicant unequivocally abandoned its rights through conduct or representation amounting to waiver.

relationships.target_case_name: Harmony Shipping Co. v Saudi Europe Line Ltd.
relationships.description: The judgment applies Lord Denning's principle that the court has a right to every person's evidence and that there is no property in a witness of fact.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Chung Ming Development Fund Co Ltd v Profit Surplus Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case, which itself applied American Cyanamid, that an injunction should not be granted if damages are an adequate remedy.

relationships.target_case_name: American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority as the source of the principle that an injunction should be refused if damages would be an adequate remedy.

relationships.target_case_name: The Incorporated Owners of Hoi To Court
relationships.description: The judgment cites Chu J's holding that inordinate delay does not itself provide a defence to an application to enforce an agreement.

candidates.case_name: CHINA MEDICAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC. (IN LIQUIDATION) AND OTHERS v. SAMSON TSANG TAK YUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Au and Chow JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court is not bound by a default rule that costs of an interlocutory injunction application should be 'in the cause'. The court should exercise its discretion by examining the merits of the injunction application itself at the time it was made, considering all circumstances, without necessarily linking the costs order to the eventual outcome of the trial.

principles.principle_label: Independent assessment of injunction merits for costs
principles.paraphrase_en: In exercising its discretion on costs for an injunction application, the court may properly assess the merits of the application independently to determine the appropriate costs order, based on the circumstances of the application itself, rather than deferring to or linking it with the final trial outcome.

candidates.case_name: ZHAO ZHI QIANG v. ZHAO ZHIGUANG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge MK Liu (Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court hearing an appeal against a judge's decision to continue or discharge an interlocutory injunction should not substitute its own discretion. It must defer to the judge's exercise of discretion and can only interfere if there was an error of law or principle, consideration of irrelevant matters, or a decision outside the reasonable bounds of discretion.

Even if a party establishes material non-disclosure by the applicant for an injunction, the court retains a discretion not to discharge the injunction. The court may consider other factors and exercise its discretion against discharge.

principles.principle_label: Finality of rulings on variation applications
principles.paraphrase_en: An application to vary a costs order nisi is not an opportunity to reargue matters already determined in the main decision. The court is not permitted to revisit those substantive rulings.

There is no fixed principle that costs of an interlocutory injunction application should be 'in the cause' by default. The court should assess the merits of the application as they stood at the time it was made, which are not necessarily dependent on the final trial outcome.

principles.principle_label: Costs follow the event for failed opposition
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a party vigorously opposes the continuation of an injunction and seeks its discharge but fails entirely, costs should generally follow the event (be awarded to the successful party).

relationships.description: The judgment cites authority for the principle that an appellate court should not exercise an independent discretion when reviewing a judge's decision on an interlocutory injunction.
The judgment cites authority confirming that the court is not allowed to revisit matters already determined in the main decision when hearing a variation application.
The judgment cites authority for the proposition that there is no default principle that costs of an interlocutory injunction should be in the cause.
The judgment cites post-Civil Justice Reform cases as examples where courts ordered continuation of an injunction and awarded costs to the plaintiff.

candidates.case_name: 郭莉 v. 刘君林
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Procedure for varying injunctions with parallel applications
principles.paraphrase_en: When an application to vary an interlocutory injunction is made alongside a parallel application for another order (such as a vesting or garnishee order), the court should adopt a procedure that ensures all interested parties have an opportunity to make submissions.

principles.principle_label: Duty to inform court of related variation applications
principles.paraphrase_en: The applicant for a substantive order (like a vesting order) should inform the court that an application to vary an existing injunction to accommodate that order has been or will be made.

relationships.target_case_name: Rong Wei v Chiu Sung Fai also known as Allan Chiu also known as Chiu Sung Fai Allan and Another
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the procedural approach established in Rong Wei for handling an application to vary an interlocutory injunction when there is a parallel application for a garnishee order.

candidates.case_name: HUI (ANNIE) GUO v. YUEDONG XU AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Reservation of rights in fee disputes
principles.paraphrase_en: A client who has a dispute over legal fees may settle the disputed bills first while expressly reserving the right to refer the bills for taxation, and subsequently commence taxation proceedings to challenge the fees.

relationships.target_case_name: Springbok Shipping (HK) Ltd & others v Clayton Wong & Co
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Court of Appeal authority to support the proposition that paying bills under a reservation of rights preserves the right to taxation.

candidates.case_name: OASIS KINDERGARTEN LTD v. KOWLOON TONG BAPTIST CHURCH
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Phoebe Man in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When a party applies to withdraw an interlocutory injunction summons, the court should normally determine the costs of that application and any previously reserved costs at that time, unless there is justification to delay such determination.

principles.principle_label: No default rule for interlocutory injunction costs
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no invariable principle or default rule that the costs of an application for an interlocutory injunction must be reserved until the conclusion of the trial or that they should normally be 'costs in the cause'.
The general rule is that a party who discontinues an action must bear the costs of the opposite party.

A discontinuing party is entitled to costs if it is the effective winner, meaning it has obtained substantially what it sought in the action or application, which displaces the Discontinuance Principle.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of effective winner for interim relief
principles.paraphrase_en: The Effective Winner Principle may not apply to an interlocutory injunction summons if the undertaking obtained is interim and the underlying proceedings remain unresolved, as final success in the main action could negate the basis for the injunction.

relationships.target_case_name: Perrine Chamonix Limited v Star League Limited and Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that a party who achieves substantially what was sought is the effective winner and entitled to costs.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Lucky Ford Industrial Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites Harris J's reasoning that if a petitioner obtains substantially what was sought, they are treated as successful for costs purposes.

relationships.target_case_name: Chow Steel Industries Public Company Limited & Ors v. Ko Sung & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Appeal's holding that there is no principle that costs of an interlocutory injunction must be in the cause and the court should look at the merits at the time of application.

candidates.case_name: RE CHU JIARU
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP and Au JA in Court

candidates.case_name: BONITA CO., LTD S.A. v. CHARK YUK YING BRENDA t/a BRENDA CHARK & CO.
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Conditions for granting leave to discontinue
principles.paraphrase_en: When granting leave to discontinue an action, the court should only impose conditions if, after a fair assessment of all relevant circumstances, such conditions are necessary either to protect the defendant or to serve the public interest.

Previous cases on imposing conditions or refusing leave to discontinue do not establish a clear universal principle; each case must be decided on its own facts, with the court applying first principles to determine what is just in all the circumstances.

The fundamental question is whether, based on a fair view of all relevant circumstances, imposing conditions is necessary for the protection of the defendant or in the public interest, with the ultimate aim of doing justice between the parties.

relationships.target_case_name: Chappie v Warrington
relationships.description: The judgment cites the principle from Chappie regarding when conditions should be imposed on discontinuance.

relationships.target_case_name: Inchroy Credit Corporation Ltd v Cheung Man-cheung
relationships.description: The judgment notes that Kaplan J cited with approval the holding from Chappie in this case.

candidates.case_name: KING FIELD SHIPYARD LTD v. TAI PAK SEA-FOOD RESTAURANT LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Phoebe Man in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A preservation order should only be granted for the purpose of maintaining the property's condition until the substantive legal issues in the case are resolved.

principles.principle_label: Limitation on Scope of Preservation Order
principles.paraphrase_en: If a preservation order is requested in a form that would cause irreversible consequences extending beyond the core purpose of preservation, this is a significant factor weighing against granting the order.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI MAN LING AND OTHERS
judges: Before: His Honour Judge W.K. Kwok

principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Final Appeal has conclusively determined that the sedition offence under section 10 of the Crimes Ordinance constitutes an offence endangering national security.

principles.principle_label: Mandatory designated judge for national security cases
principles.paraphrase_en: For cases concerning offences endangering national security, the National Security Law mandates that bail applications and trials must be presided over by a designated judge.

An offence classified as endangering national security at the bail stage must retain that same classification throughout the trial proceedings; inconsistent classification within the same case is not permissible.

principles.principle_label: Proper procedure for listing before designated judge
principles.paraphrase_en: It is proper procedure for the prosecution to request that a case involving a national security offence be listed before a designated judge, as required by law.

principles.principle_label: Request for designated judge not judge-shopping
principles.paraphrase_en: A prosecution request for a case to be heard by a designated judge, as mandated for national security offences, does not constitute improper judge-shopping when no particular judge is specified.

relationships.target_case_name: Court of Final Appeal ruling
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the CFA's conclusive determination that sedition under section 10 of the Crimes Ordinance is an offence endangering national security.

candidates.case_name: RE CHINA ZENITH CHEMICAL GROUP LTD (formerly known as XINYANG MAOJIAN GROUP LTD)
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP and Barma JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A company seeking to resist a winding-up petition must demonstrate a genuine dispute on substantial grounds regarding the petitioner's debt.

relationships.target_case_name: Judgment of Recorder Jenkin Suen SC dated 10 October 2024
relationships.description: The present appeal is brought against this lower court judgment which ordered the winding-up petition to proceed.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOW HANG TUNG
judges: Before: Hon Alex Lee, Johnny Chan & Anna Lai, JJ in Court

principles.principle_label: Equality of arms in fair trial
principles.paraphrase_en: The Basic Law and Hong Kong Bill of Rights guarantee equality before the law and a fair trial, which includes the right of an accused to call witnesses under the same conditions as the prosecution.

principles.principle_label: Restriction on video-link evidence in national security cases
principles.paraphrase_en: Amendments under section 144 apply equally to both prosecution and defense in national security proceedings by prohibiting overseas witnesses from giving evidence via television link, maintaining equality of arms.

principles.principle_label: Primary principle of witness presence in court
principles.paraphrase_en: In criminal proceedings, witnesses must generally give evidence in person before the court unless both parties agree to written statements or admitted facts, with video-link evidence being an exception requiring court leave.

In national security cases, measures must be taken to preclude national security risks, such as preventing potential coaching of witnesses via live television link that could pervert the course of justice.

Restrictions on the mode of calling witnesses in national security cases constitute limited regulation that does not affect the substantive right to call witnesses or prejudice the right to a fair trial.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Hon Ming Kong & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Appeal's explanation that equality of arms requires the accused to have the same conditions as the prosecution for calling witnesses.

candidates.case_name: R v. C
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Jonathan Wong in Chambers (Not open to Public) (Paper Disposal)

principles.principle_label: Security for costs against foreign parties
principles.paraphrase_en: The mere fact that a party is based outside Hong Kong is not, by itself, a sufficient ground to order security for costs in arbitration proceedings. Parties choose Hong Kong-seated arbitrations for its neutral and efficient dispute resolution, and should not be penalized for this choice.

principles.principle_label: Timing of security application in arbitration
principles.paraphrase_en: An application for security for costs made during a challenge to an arbitrator (post-award) is distinct from one made under statutory provisions before the tribunal is constituted, as it occurs after the consensual dispute resolution process has concluded.

A court may take judicial notice of the obvious practical realities regarding the enforcement of an award, such as a party's obstructive conduct or history of non-compliance, even in the absence of formal evidence on the point.

relationships.target_case_name: Ukraine v Pao Tatneft
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case as an example where security for costs was granted against a state, considering factors like a history of obdurate opposition to enforcement.

relationships.target_case_name: Nasser v The United Bank of Kuwait
relationships.description: The present judgment references this authority for the proposition that courts can take notice of obvious realities in a case, even without formal evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: Eiser Infrastructure Ltd v Kingdom of Spain
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case in the context of recognizing and enforcing an investment treaty arbitration award against a state.

candidates.case_name: CORNELIS PIETER ALEXANDER VAN DE RIET v. KD CONCEPT LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Joseph Vaughan in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Security for costs against foreign plaintiff
principles.paraphrase_en: It is generally unfair to allow a foreign plaintiff, who is effectively immune from enforcement of a costs order if unsuccessful, to proceed without providing security for costs within the jurisdiction.

principles.principle_label: Merits assessment threshold in security for costs
principles.paraphrase_en: The court should be very cautious about assessing the merits of a case on paper for a security for costs application, and should only do so when the merits are very clear, as the threshold for demonstrating a probability of success is very high.

principles.principle_label: Discretionary nature of security for costs
principles.paraphrase_en: The court has a discretion in security for costs applications to do what is fair and just in all the circumstances of the case.

principles.principle_label: Counterclaim's effect on security for costs
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a defendant has a counterclaim that exceeds the plaintiff's claim, it may be unfair and unjust to order the plaintiff to provide security for costs.

If a counterclaim involves independent factual and legal issues from the main claim, this may not be a complete bar to security for costs, but may instead justify an apportionment of the security required.

relationships.target_case_name: Tagliani v Lee Wai Ying Elvis
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Tagliani that it is prima facie unjust for a foreign plaintiff immune from costs enforcement to proceed without providing security.

relationships.target_case_name: Lim Yi Shen v. Wong Yuen Yee
relationships.description: The judgment applies Recorder Horace Wong SC's caution against assessing merits on paper in security for costs applications unless the case is very clear.

relationships.target_case_name: BJ Crabtree (Insulation) Ltd v GPT Communication Systems Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that a counterclaim exceeding the plaintiff's claim may make it unfair to order security for costs.

relationships.target_case_name: Hutchinson Telephone (UK) Ltd v Ultimate Response Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the discretionary principle from this case that the court must do what is fair and just in all circumstances when considering security for costs.

candidates.case_name: REAL ESTATE AND FINANCE FUND (IN LIQUIDATION) AND ANOTHER v. SUN CHEUK PAK ALAN AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Mr Recorder Maurellet, SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing whether proposed security is adequate, the court must consider the nature and characteristics of the security, including its market capitalisation, management, trading volume, and how it is held, compared to conventional security like a bank guarantee or payment into court.

principles.principle_label: Estoppel by prior representation on asset value
principles.paraphrase_en: A party may be prevented from arguing that an asset is not good security due to price fluctuation or illiquidity if they have previously, in a related application (such as to discharge a Mareva injunction), pointed to that same asset as a substantial asset of significant or stable value.

candidates.case_name: DELCO PARTICIPATION B.V. v. HWH HOLDINGS LTD
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Real attacker in security for costs
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering an application for security for costs where a counterclaim exists, the court must determine which party is the 'real attacker' in the proceedings.

The amount of security for costs is at the court's discretion, which must fix a just sum considering all circumstances; it is not standard practice to order security on a full indemnity basis, and a discount may be appropriate.

A counterclaim that arises from a defence of equitable set-off or estoppel by convention operates as a defence itself, not merely as a cross-claim, and its balance is the arithmetic result of that defence.
An order for security for costs does not necessarily have to provide full security, and the quantum is often based on estimates, especially depending on the procedural stage.

relationships.target_case_name: Ai Zhong case
relationships.description: The judgment references Ai Zhong for the principle of identifying the 'real attacker' in security for costs applications involving counterclaims.

candidates.case_name: RE SNG ALLAN, also known as SNG HOCK SENG
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and Barma JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Courts must adopt a contextual and purposive approach to statutory interpretation, giving effect to legislative intent while considering the natural and ordinary meaning of the language and avoiding distortion of the statutory provisions.

principles.principle_label: Nil valuation of security in bankruptcy
principles.paraphrase_en: A petitioner in bankruptcy proceedings may validly estimate the value of their security interest as nil under section 6B of the Bankruptcy Ordinance, particularly when the security has declined in value, become worthless, or is a subordinate charge on property with insufficient value.

There is no conceptual barrier under section 6B to treating the secured portion of a debt as having zero value, and the statute's reference to 'parts' of a debt does not require the secured part to exceed zero.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Furgo Geotechnical Services Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment follows the statutory interpretation principles established in Furgo Geotechnical Services Ltd.

relationships.target_case_name: Re O'Leary, ex parte Bayne
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Australian authority regarding the valuation of security interests in bankruptcy.

candidates.case_name: P1 AND ANOTHER v. D
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Jonathan Wong in Chambers (Not open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Discretion on security for costs for foreign plaintiffs
principles.paraphrase_en: The court has complete discretion to order security for costs against a plaintiff resident abroad; there is no rigid rule or presumption for or against such an order. The court must consider all circumstances to determine if it is just.

principles.principle_label: Non-discriminatory nature of security for costs rule
principles.paraphrase_en: Order 23, rule 1(1)(a) of the Rules of the High Court, which allows security for costs against a plaintiff resident out of jurisdiction, is not discriminatory or potentially discriminatory.

principles.principle_label: Factors favouring security for costs in arbitration
principles.paraphrase_en: In arbitration, if there is a real risk that a party will have difficulty enforcing a costs award due to the other party's lack of accessible assets, this favours an order for security, unless this risk was an accepted business risk at the start of the relationship.

If a party's lack of funds or assets results from deliberate actions to hide assets, provide wrong information, or frustrate a future costs award (rather than accepted business risk), this is a factor favouring an order for security for costs.

principles.principle_label: Consequence of failing to provide security
principles.paraphrase_en: It is not objectionable in principle for a court order to provide for dismissal of an application if the plaintiff fails to provide the ordered security for costs, as an alternative to merely staying the proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: Keary Developments Ltd v Tarmac Construction Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that the court has complete discretion regarding security for costs for plaintiffs resident abroad.

relationships.target_case_name: Tagliani v Lee Wai Ying Elvis
relationships.description: The judgment applies this authority to support the holding that the security for costs rule is not discriminatory.

relationships.target_case_name: Lim Yi Shenn v Wong Yuen Yee
relationships.description: The judgment references this case as cited in Hong Kong Civil Procedure to support the non-discriminatory nature of the security for costs rule.

relationships.target_case_name: Soleh Boneh v Government of Uganda
relationships.description: The judgment notes this case sets out the 'manifest validity' test for assessing the merits of an award in the context of security for costs applications.

relationships.target_case_name: Wang Hsuan Han v Cathay Pacific Airways Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies this authority to support that dismissal (not just a stay) is a permissible consequence for failing to provide ordered security.

candidates.case_name: CORNELIS PIETER ALEXANDER v. KD CONCEPT LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Joseph Vaughan in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge who has already dismissed an application on its merits becomes functus officio and lacks the power to reconsider the substantive merits of that same application.

Points that could have been argued at the original hearing cannot be raised later to vary an order after it has been made and sealed, especially without providing a satisfactory explanation for the delay.

relationships.target_case_name: Andayani v Chan Oi Ling
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in Andayani that a judge becomes functus officio after deciding an application on its merits.

relationships.target_case_name: Nguyen Quang Hien v Torture Claims Appeal Board / Non-Refoulement Claims Petition Office
relationships.description: The judgment applies the observation from Nguyen Quang Hien regarding a judge's lack of power to reconsider the merits of a dismissed application.

relationships.target_case_name: Zhu Kuan Co. of Zhu Hai Sez v Brickell Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites the unreported decision in Zhu Kuan Co. as additional authority supporting the functus officio principle.

candidates.case_name: LAI CHEE-YING v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An interpretation of the National Security Law issued by the NPCSC is, in substance and practical effect, equivalent to an interpretation of the Basic Law issued by the NPCSC.

relationships.target_case_name: Lau Kong Yung v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the proposition regarding the substantive equivalence of NPCSC interpretations.

relationships.target_case_name: Chief Executive of the HKSAR v President of the Legislative Council
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to further support the principle concerning the status of NPCSC interpretations.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG HAU YI SIDNEY (伍巧怡)
judges: Appeal Committee: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ and Mr Justice Fok PJ

principles.paraphrase_en: The more stringent bail threshold under Article 42(2) of the National Security Law applies not only to offences created by the NSL itself, but also to acts endangering national security that are capable of constituting an offence under the NSL or under other HKSAR laws that safeguard national security.

The phrase 'acts endangering national security' in NSL Article 42(2) should be construed broadly to cover acts capable of constituting an offence under the National Security Law or under other HKSAR laws enacted to safeguard national security.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Lai Chee Ying
relationships.description: The present judgment follows and applies the construction of NSL Article 42(2) established in the Lai case.

candidates.case_name: LX v. CWC, the Administratrix of the Estate of WTFP
judges: Coram: HH Judge C.K. Chan in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The court has jurisdiction to order security for costs against a plaintiff who is ordinarily resident outside of Hong Kong.

principles.principle_label: Governing rules for security for costs
principles.paraphrase_en: Applications for security for costs are governed by Order 23 of the Rules of the District Court.

candidates.case_name: ZLL AND ANOTHER v. CBC
judges: Coram: HH Judge C.K. Chan in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Security for costs against parent in minor's case
principles.paraphrase_en: Security for costs should only be ordered against a parent involved in proceedings concerning minors in the most exceptional circumstances, based on the principle that parents should be able to present their case regarding the child's welfare without financial impediment.

principles.principle_label: Threshold for security for costs application
principles.paraphrase_en: An application for security for costs fails where the applicant's underlying claim demonstrates a high probability of success on liability.

relationships.target_case_name: Re B (Infants)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Re B (Infants) that security for costs against a parent in a minor's case requires exceptional circumstances.

relationships.target_case_name: RM v SRM (Security for Costs: Child Relocation)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Hong Kong decision in RM v SRM, which itself applied the principle from Re B (Infants).

candidates.case_name: SA AND OTHERS v. BH AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Under the Arbitration Ordinance, the court may only intervene in arbitration matters as expressly permitted by the legislation.

principles.principle_label: Security for costs in award challenges
principles.paraphrase_en: The court possesses jurisdiction to order security for costs from a party seeking to set aside an arbitral award made in Hong Kong.

principles.principle_label: No distinction between setting aside and opposing enforcement for security
principles.paraphrase_en: The principles for granting security for costs apply equally whether a party actively seeks to set aside an award or passively opposes its enforcement; the distinction is not material.

relationships.target_case_name: 中國機床銷售與技術服務有限公司 v 國晟機電設備有限公司 (Nationsync Electrical and Machinery Equip Corp Limited)
relationships.description: The present judgment agrees with and follows the reasoning in this case regarding the applicability of security for costs principles.

relationships.target_case_name: Soleh Boneh v Government of Uganda
relationships.description: The judgment applies the established principles from this case for granting security in the context of challenges to arbitral awards.

candidates.case_name: HIGH FASHION NEW MEDIA CORPORATION LTD (suing on behalf of itself and also in its capacity as the sole shareholder of LONGFORD INFORMATION AND TECHNOLOGY CO LTD) v. LEONG MA LI
judges: Before: Hon Barma JA and Au JA in Court

candidates.case_name: SONG LIHUA v. LEE CHEE HON (Former Name: QUE WENBIN) AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge KC Chan in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The correct approach when deciding an application for a temporary stay of proceedings is to consider the balance of convenience and fairness between the parties, and the court should exercise its discretion accordingly.

The court may decline a proposed hearing arrangement if it would require the parties to expend unnecessary time and costs preparing for substantive arguments on an application that might not ultimately proceed.

principles.principle_label: Principle of imposing terms despite small amount
principles.paraphrase_en: As a matter of principle, a court may impose a term or condition even if the financial amount involved is not substantial, where fairness requires it.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Chime Corporation Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment accepts and applies the approach to temporary stay applications as held by Kwan J (as VP then was) in Re Chime Corporation Ltd.

relationships.target_case_name: Alfred McAlpine Construction Ltd. v. Unex Corporation Ltd.
relationships.description: The judgment notes that the approach in Re Chime Corporation Ltd derived from the principle stated by Glidewell LJ in Alfred McAlpine regarding the balance of convenience and fairness.

relationships.target_case_name: Clinton Engineering Ltd. v. B-Tech (Holdings) Ltd.
relationships.description: The judgment notes that the Alfred McAlpine approach was applied in this Hong Kong case, as referenced in Re Chime Corporation Ltd.

relationships.target_case_name: SWE Ltd. v. Chong Lai Fun
relationships.description: The judgment notes this case as another instance where the Alfred McAlpine approach was applied, as referenced in Re Chime Corporation Ltd.

candidates.case_name: TWO WAY MEDIA LTD v. PCCW LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Lok J in Chambers

candidates.case_name: AAD AND ANOTHER v. BBF
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Reyes, SC in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Security against respondent in commercial disputes
principles.paraphrase_en: In a purely commercial dispute, a court will generally not order a party in the position of a respondent to provide security as a condition for defending or resisting a claim or application, due to fundamental principles of access to justice and the right to defend oneself unconditionally.

principles.principle_label: Choice of remedies in cross-border arbitration
principles.paraphrase_en: In cross-border arbitration, the losing party has a choice between an active remedy (applying to set aside the award at the seat) and a passive remedy (resisting recognition and enforcement in the jurisdiction where enforcement is sought). This choice is a recognized principle that bolsters access to justice.

principles.principle_label: Disobedience of court order as a bar to being heard
principles.paraphrase_en: A party's disobedience of a court order is not an automatic bar to being heard. However, if the disobedience impedes the course of justice by hindering the court's ability to ascertain the truth or enforce its orders, the court may in its discretion refuse to hear that party until the impediment is removed or good reason is shown.

principles.principle_label: Security for costs and peremptory orders
principles.paraphrase_en: In security for costs applications, courts must take a prima facie view of the merits of each side's case. A typical order stays proceedings until security is paid, but a peremptory order that strikes out an application unless security is paid by a specific date requires careful consideration.

relationships.target_case_name: Astro Nusantara
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Astro Nusantara that a losing party in arbitration has a choice between active and passive remedies.

relationships.target_case_name: Dana Shipping
relationships.description: The judgment notes the claimants' submission relies on factors identified by Mimmie Chan J in Dana Shipping.

candidates.case_name: WANG HSUAN HAN v. CATHAY PACIFIC AIRWAYS LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge H. Au-Yeung in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Security for costs against foreign plaintiffs
principles.paraphrase_en: It is generally unfair to allow a foreign plaintiff, who is effectively protected from costs enforcement if they lose, to proceed with litigation without first providing security within the jurisdiction to cover potential adverse costs orders.

principles.principle_label: Merits assessment in security for costs applications
principles.paraphrase_en: Courts should generally avoid assessing the substantive merits of a case on paper in security for costs applications, particularly where facts depend on oral evidence. The threshold for showing a probability of success in such applications is very high, and delving into the merits is undesirable unless the case is clearly strong or weak.

principles.principle_label: Costs of adjournment caused by a party
principles.paraphrase_en: Where an adjournment is caused solely by one party, that party should generally bear the costs of the adjournment as a matter of principle.

relationships.target_case_name: Tagliani v Lee Wai Ying Elvis
relationships.description: The present judgment cites Tagliani for the principle that it is prima facie unjust for a foreign plaintiff immune from costs enforcement to proceed without providing security.

relationships.target_case_name: Lim Yi Shen v. Wong Yuen Yee
relationships.description: The present judgment cites Lim Yi Shen for the caution against assessing merits on paper in security for costs applications and the high threshold for demonstrating probability of success.

candidates.case_name: LAM WAI LIK ERIC AND OTHERS v. TAM SUET MUI
judges: Before: Hon Au and Chow JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Security for costs for foreign appellants
principles.paraphrase_en: A party residing outside the jurisdiction is prima facie required to provide security for the costs of an appeal, absent any countervailing factors that would make such an order inappropriate.

principles.principle_label: Court's discretion on form of security
principles.paraphrase_en: The court has discretion to determine the appropriate form of security for costs of an appeal where it is considered that security should be given.
cited_statutes: Order 59, r 10(5) of the Rules of the High Court

principles.principle_label: Purpose of security for costs order
principles.paraphrase_en: An order for security for costs should aim to provide reasonable, but not absolute, protection for the costs of the party to whom security is to be given.

candidates.case_name: RE DRAGON SEAFOODS LTD
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP and Harris J in Court

principles.principle_label: Extension of time for relief from sanction
principles.paraphrase_en: An application to extend time for seeking relief from a sanction (such as dismissal for non-compliance) must be made promptly and requires the court to consider the reasons for the delay and the merits of the underlying application for relief.

Where an order for security for costs includes a self-executing sanction that the appeal will be dismissed if security is not provided within a specified time, the appeal is automatically dismissed upon default without the need for a further court order.

candidates.case_name: GURDAS S. CHOITHRAMANI v. MAYER BROWN (formerly known as MAYER BROWN JSM formerly known as JOHNSON STOKES & MASTER) AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP and Chow JA in Court

candidates.case_name: HWH HOLDINGS LTD AND ANOTHER v. STEPHENSON HARWOOD (A FIRM)
judges: Before: Hon Barma and G Lam JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Joint and several liability for legal costs
principles.paraphrase_en: Multiple parties can be held jointly and severally liable for legal bills incurred, meaning the creditor can seek payment from any one or all of them.

principles.principle_label: Execution against escrow funds subject to Mareva
principles.paraphrase_en: A costs order can, in principle, be executed against funds held in escrow even if those funds are subject to a Mareva injunction, provided the injunction does not specifically prohibit such execution.
An order for costs on an indemnity basis, rather than the standard basis, requires the demonstration of sufficient special features in the case to justify it.
The court may summarily assess costs using a broad-brush approach, considering the submitted statement of costs, without requiring a detailed taxation.

candidates.case_name: LAI CHEE YING v. THE COMMITTEE FOR SAFEGUARDING NATIONAL SECURITY OF THE HKSAR AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Chu VP and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The Hong Kong courts have not been vested with any jurisdiction to supervise or review the work of the National Security Committee under Article 14 of the National Security Law, and therefore the question of that article ousting the courts' supervisory jurisdiction does not arise.

An interpretation issued by the Standing Committee of the National People's Congress regarding the National Security Law has the same legal effect as the Law itself from the date the Law came into effect, and the Hong Kong courts are bound to follow it under the 'one country, two systems' principle.

There exists a strong interpretative presumption against the statutory exclusion of judicial review, which can only be displaced by the most clear and explicit statutory wording, as judicial review is a principal mechanism for upholding the rule of law.

principles.principle_label: Rule of law and judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: The right to access a court to review the lawfulness of administrative or executive acts is essential to the rule of law, which requires, at a minimum, review for jurisdictional error that renders a decision a nullity.

principles.principle_label: Discretion on indemnity costs on appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court has the discretion to decide whether to award indemnity costs on appeal, and may decline to do so even if they were awarded below, particularly where the appeal raises distinct or reasonable legal arguments.

relationships.description: The judgment references the principle that statutory exclusion of judicial review requires 'the most clear and explicit words', a well-established common law presumption.

candidates.case_name: UNITED HARVEST INDUSTRIES LTD v. CHING MAN CHUN LOUIS AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Alan Kwong in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: MA CHUN MAN v. COMMISSIONER OF CORRECTIONAL SERVICES
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC, Pang and Anthea Pang JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The constitutional right to liberty under the Hong Kong Bill of Rights Article 5(1) is not automatically engaged by changes to remission rules or administrative decisions regarding expected release dates.
Arguments or grounds not pleaded in the originating documents or raised before the trial judge cannot be relied upon for the first time on appeal.
Findings of fact and legal rulings made by the trial judge that are not appealed become final and limit the scope of the appeal to the remaining contested issues.

candidates.case_name: LEE SAU YEE SHIRLEY v. RYBINSKI MARIUSZ
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Tony Ko in Chambers
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.paraphrase_en: For an interim payment order, the court must first be satisfied that if the case proceeds to trial, the applicant would obtain judgment for a substantial sum of money.

When considering an application for interim payment, the court should read Order 29 rules 11 and 12 together and ask a single question about whether the applicant fulfills the requirements of those rules as a whole, rather than examining each rule separately and exclusively.

relationships.target_case_name: Shearson Lehman Brothers Inc v. Maclaine, Watson & Co. Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the approach from Shearson Lehman Brothers regarding the interpretation of Order 29 rules 11 and 12 for interim payment applications.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BUI VAN-CUONG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and Anthea Pang JJA in Court
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.paraphrase_en: For the defense of provocation to arise, there must be provoking conduct that caused the defendant to lose self-control. If no such provocation exists, the defense fails and the defendant is guilty of murder.

When assessing provocation, the jury must consider whether a person of ordinary self-control would have reacted as the accused did, taking into account the accused's entire factual situation including their history, experiences, background, and personal attributes.
The law of provocation should not indirectly incorporate the concept of diminished responsibility, as these are distinct legal defenses with different requirements and purposes.

relationships.target_case_name: Camplin
relationships.description: The judgment references Lord Simon's approach in Camplin regarding consideration of the 'entire factual situation' when assessing provocation.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: YIP YUEN CHUN SHIREA v. CHICK TAK HIM in his capacity as executor of the estate of CHICK TAK HONG PETER
judges: Before: Hon Leung J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Contractual ousting of statutory succession rules
principles.paraphrase_en: Parties to a deed of family arrangement can agree that their entitlements to an estate are to be determined solely by the terms of that deed, thereby ousting the application of statutory succession laws, intestacy rules, or customs.

principles.principle_label: Binding nature of deed of family arrangement
principles.paraphrase_en: A deed of family arrangement can constitute a final and binding settlement where parties agree that their rights and interests in an estate are to be determined exclusively by the deed, and no claims can be made outside its terms.

principles.principle_label: Executor's duty to provide inventory and account
principles.paraphrase_en: An executor has a duty to provide a full inventory and account of the estate's assets, including assets held on trust for the estate, to the beneficiaries upon request.

principles.principle_label: Costs from estate for administration proceedings
principles.paraphrase_en: Where proceedings are properly brought for the benefit of a trust fund or a deceased's estate, the court may order the costs of those proceedings to be paid from the fund or estate.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Buckton
relationships.description: The judgment cites Re Buckton to explain the principle that costs of proceedings for the benefit of an estate may be borne by the estate.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: PANG YU HIM v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Ko in Chambers (Open to Public)
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Mens rea for misfeasance in public office
principles.paraphrase_en: The mental element required for the tort of misfeasance in public office is subjective. It can be established through three variants: (a) specific intent to injure the plaintiff (targeted malice), (b) knowledge that injury would follow in the ordinary course (untargeted malice), or (c) reckless indifference as to whether the actions would cause injury.
Proof of material damage is a necessary element of the tort of misfeasance in public office. The tort is not actionable without such proof, maintaining a rule established for centuries.

Exemplary damages may be awarded for misfeasance in public office where compensatory damages are insufficient to mark the court's disapproval and deter repetition, but the general policy of law does not encourage such awards, and tort law should not be developed into an instrument of punishment absent material damage.

principles.principle_label: Heads of damage for malicious prosecution
principles.paraphrase_en: In an action for malicious prosecution, damages may be claimed under three heads: damage to the person, damage to property, and damage to reputation, a rule established in common law.

principles.principle_label: Relationship between Kuddus and Watkins on exemplary damages
principles.paraphrase_en: The decision in Kuddus that exemplary damages can in principle be awarded for misfeasance in public office is not inconsistent with Watkins, which clarified that material damage is a prerequisite for the tort to be actionable.

relationships.target_case_name: Tang Nin Mun
relationships.description: The judgment cites Tang Nin Mun for the observation that the House of Lords in Three Rivers established the subjective mental element for misfeasance in public office.

relationships.target_case_name: Three Rivers
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Three Rivers that the state of mind for misfeasance in public office is subjective.

relationships.target_case_name: Watkins
relationships.description: The judgment follows Watkins in holding that proof of material damage is a necessary ingredient of the tort of misfeasance in public office.

relationships.target_case_name: Savile v Roberts
relationships.description: The judgment cites Savile v Roberts for the established rule on the three heads of damage in malicious prosecution actions.

relationships.target_case_name: Kuddus
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes Kuddus, noting it is not inconsistent with Watkins, as Kuddus dealt with exemplary damages while Watkins clarified the material damage requirement.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: WU TANGLUN v. CK ENGINEERING (HONG KONG) CO LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Phillis Loh in Court
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Employer and principal contractor joint liability
principles.paraphrase_en: An employer and a principal contractor can be held jointly and severally liable for injuries caused to a worker when an unsafe place and system of work, posing reasonably foreseeable risks, results in an accident.
A defendant bears full liability for an accident when no evidence is presented to establish any contributory negligence on the part of the injured plaintiff.

A plaintiff's pre-accident earnings should be assessed based on credible documentary evidence, such as earnings records, and the court may reject unsupported alternative figures put forward by the defendant.

principles.principle_label: Basis for loss of earning capacity award
principles.paraphrase_en: An award for loss of earning capacity is appropriate where a plaintiff, despite returning to work, suffers from residual disabilities that create a disadvantage in the open labor market, following established legal principles.

relationships.target_case_name: Yu Kok Wing v Lee Tim Loi
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the legal principles for awarding loss of earning capacity as established in this Court of Appeal case.

relationships.target_case_name: Moeliker v A Reyrolle & Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this English authority, which was referenced in Yu Kok Wing, as part of the well-established principles on loss of earning capacity.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: KUNG KWOK WAI DAVID, the executor of the last Will of KUNG WONG SAU HIN, deceased v. THE COMMISSIONER OF ESTATE DUTY
judges: Before: Hon Chow JA (sitting as an additional judge of the Court of First Instance) in Court
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for estate duty deductions
principles.paraphrase_en: The taxpayer bears the burden of proving that a claimed deduction from an estate's value, such as a loan, qualifies under the relevant statutory provision. This requires adducing sufficient documentary evidence to substantiate the claim.

principles.principle_label: Valuation methodology for property with adverse possession claims
principles.paraphrase_en: When valuing property subject to adverse possession claims, the court must assess the merits of each individual claim to determine the appropriate discount to apply to the affected lot's value, rather than applying a blanket discount.

For a single lot comprising different land use types (e.g., agricultural and building land), each portion should be valued according to its specific use and characteristics, even if the entire lot is subject to a unified legal claim like adverse possession.

principles.principle_label: Expert evidence requirement for valuation discounts
principles.paraphrase_en: An expert valuer proposing a specific discount rate (e.g., for a share disability) must provide objective evidence to support that rate; merely citing past cases with different rates is insufficient without explaining the underlying principles for the chosen figure.

principles.principle_label: Court's duty to assess market value
principles.paraphrase_en: In the absence of a legal bar (like estoppel), the court retains the ultimate duty to independently assess the market value of an asset as of the valuation date, regardless of challenges to the credibility of a party's explanation for disputing a prior valuation.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI KAN YAU
judges: Before: Hon Yau J
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.paraphrase_en: The actus reus of murder is established when the defendant's unlawful act causes the death of the victim, as demonstrated by the fatal wound being the direct cause of death.

The mental element for murder requires an intention to kill or cause grievous bodily harm. The defendant's use of a knife to inflict multiple stab wounds to a vital area is evidence from which such intent can be inferred.
Confessions made under caution in video-recorded interviews and re-enactments are admissible as evidence against the defendant, provided they are voluntary.

Expert opinion from a pathologist and toxicologist is admissible to establish the cause of death and the likely physical state of the victim (e.g., unconsciousness) at the time of the fatal act, based on forensic findings and toxicology reports.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. THAPA PREMKALA
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Bruce SC in Chambers
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.paraphrase_en: Testimony from a witness about an out-of-court statement made by a co-accused, offered to assert the truth of its contents, is inadmissible as hearsay when considered in isolation.

principles.principle_label: Bail pending appeal - substantial sentence served
principles.paraphrase_en: Where there is a substantial risk that a significant part or all of a sentence will be served before an appeal is heard, the appellant must demonstrate reasonable prospects of success on appeal to be granted bail.

While it is highly desirable for a magistrate to give reasons for conviction at the time, there is no constitutional or statutory rule that a failure to do so automatically invalidates the conviction. However, giving reasons is a component of a fair trial.

principles.principle_label: Appellate review of reasons and afterthought
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court may treat the omission of a key point in oral reasons and its later insertion in a written Statement of Findings as an irregularity, suggesting an attempt to revisit evidence or defend original findings after the fact.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of good character and silence
principles.paraphrase_en: A failure by a trial judge to recognize that no adverse inference should be drawn from an accused's decision not to testify, and to properly consider the accused's previous good character as relevant to propensity, can constitute a material error.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Lau Man Kin
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle from Lau Man Kin that an appellant must show reasonable prospects of success for bail when a substantial part of the sentence may be served before appeal.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Lai Kam Wai
relationships.description: The judgment cites Lai Kam Wai for the proposition that a magistrate's failure to give immediate reasons does not automatically vitiate a conviction.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Chan Ping Sing
relationships.description: The judgment cites Chan Ping Sing as further authority supporting the principle that a lack of immediate reasons from a magistrate does not nullify a conviction.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Siti Handayani
relationships.description: The judgment applies the approach from Siti Handayani regarding the importance of appropriately considering an accused's good character.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ALI ZAIN
judges: Before : H.H. Judge G. Lam
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Duty of care at pedestrian crossings
principles.paraphrase_en: Motorists have a legal duty to exercise the highest degree of care when approaching pedestrian crossings, due to the foreseeable presence of pedestrians at any time.
The courts must send a clear message that they treat traffic accidents occurring at pedestrian crossings with the utmost seriousness.

relationships.target_case_name: SJ v Lam Siu Tong
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the principle from this case that motorists must exercise utmost care at pedestrian crossings.

relationships.target_case_name: SJ v Wong Wai Hung
relationships.description: The judgment applies the judicial comment from this case that courts must treat pedestrian crossing accidents very seriously.

judges: Before: Deputy District Judge B Mak in Chambers (Open to Public)
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: CSF v. HYC
judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge F. Li in Chambers (By paper disposal)
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Grounds for leave to appeal - 'some other reason'
principles.paraphrase_en: The 'some other reason' ground for granting leave to appeal is not limited to an exhaustive list of circumstances; it can be invoked when the Court of Appeal finds it in the interests of justice, such as when the case involves a question of general principle being decided for the first time, or a question of importance where further appellate consideration would benefit the public.
The legal test for admitting new evidence after trial for the purpose of an appeal is governed by established authority, which would be applied if a formal application is made.

relationships.target_case_name: Ladd v Marshall
relationships.description: The judgment cites Ladd v Marshall as the well-established authority governing the admissibility of new evidence on appeal.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. EKSTROM PATRIK TOBIAS
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and Barnes J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of electronic messages as recent complaint
principles.paraphrase_en: Electronic messages, such as WhatsApp messages, can be admissible as recent complaint evidence even if the recipient of those messages does not testify in court.

The requirement for a complaint to be 'recent' involves not only a temporal assessment but also a broader contextual and subjective evaluation, considering factors like the nature of the assault, the relationship with the assailant, and the complainant's characteristics.

For a communication to qualify as a complaint of a sexual offence, it must at least convey to the recipient that a sexual wrong by the accused is being alleged, though it need not recite all the legal ingredients of the offence.

When determining if conduct qualifies as a complaint, the court must consider the whole circumstances holistically; factors like consistency with the later account can be part of this assessment and need not be considered in rigid isolation.

An appellate court should be slow to overturn a verdict based on a challenge to tactical decisions made by trial counsel, unless there are exceptional circumstances or a demonstrable miscarriage of justice.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v So Tsz Yeung
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that WhatsApp messages can be admissible as recent complaint evidence even if the recipient does not testify.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Chak Kong Fai
relationships.description: The judgment applies the modern legal guidance from this case on the contextual factors for determining what constitutes a 'recent' complaint.

relationships.target_case_name: R v S
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case alongside Chak Kong Fai for guidance on the law concerning recent complaints in sexual offence cases.

relationships.target_case_name: Lam Siu Fung Andy
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that appellate courts should be reluctant to interfere with verdicts based on trial counsel's tactical decisions.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Birks
relationships.description: The judgment applies the established principle from this case concerning appellate restraint regarding trial counsel's tactical choices.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. LAI CHEE YING
judges: Before: Hon Andrew Chan J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Appeal by Case Stated vs rehearing
principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal under Section 105 of the Magistrates Ordinance (by way of Case Stated) is distinct from an appeal under Section 113 (by way of rehearing). The appellate court in a Case Stated appeal does not reassess the weight of trial evidence, including witness testimony; it is a review.

A magistrate's finding of fact constitutes an error of law (perversity) if no reasonable magistrate, properly directing themselves, could have reached that conclusion. This requires showing the magistrate misdirected themselves, misunderstood facts, considered irrelevant matters, or overlooked relevant ones.

For criminal intimidation, the key questions are the intention behind the threat and its effect (or likely effect) on the recipient. This is assessed by reference to the specific circumstances of both persons involved at the time the words were spoken, regardless of whether the threat was made directly, reported later, or not reported at all.

The court must consider whether a threat is of a kind likely to overcome the ordinary free will of a person of ordinary firmness. The actual alarm of the recipient is a relevant factor, but the test is objective (the 'firm man'), not purely subjective.
A suspect's denial or rejection of a proposition during a police interview cannot, without more, be treated as an admission or confession of that same proposition.

relationships.target_case_name: Lo Tong Kai v The Queen
relationships.description: The judgment applies the legal principle from Lo Tong Kai regarding the relevant questions for criminal intimidation (intention and effect of the threat).

relationships.target_case_name: Kwan Sing Kit
relationships.description: The judgment cites Kwan Sing Kit as a comparable case where the parties had a long history of conflict, which is relevant to assessing the context of the threat.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: ANTHONY ERIC RYAN HOTUNG v. HOTUNG, MICHAEL ERIC A.B. MAK SHUN MING, the Executor of the Estate of HO YUEN KI WINNIE, also known as HO YUEN KI, deceased
judges: Before: Hon Kwan Ag CJHC, Au JA and Chow JA in Court
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.paraphrase_en: A party is bound by their pleaded case and cannot pursue claims or allegations not properly pleaded, even if referenced in later evidence.
A claimant must prove that actual loss flowed from a breach of duty; failure to do so results in only nominal damages being awarded.

principles.principle_label: Trustee's duty to preserve trust property
principles.paraphrase_en: A trustee's duty to preserve trust property is not negated by a settlor's condition that trustees should not interfere with the management of the company whose shares are held in trust.
Even for claims of breach of fiduciary duty, the court must inquire what difference the breach made; the claimant must show that loss would not have occurred but for the breach.
