If the resisting party demonstrates a real prospect of successfully setting aside the enforcement order, immediate enforcement or a substantial security order is not appropriate.

A party resisting enforcement of a foreign arbitral award has a real prospect of success if it can establish grounds such as the invalidity of the underlying arbitration agreement, improper notice of the arbitration proceedings, or a failure in disclosure during the arbitration.

candidates.case_name: 深圳市智能制造軟件開發有限公司 v. YICK HEUNG KNITTERS LTD
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Formal requirements for setting aside enforcement orders
principles.paraphrase_en: An application to set aside an order granting leave to enforce an arbitral award must be made by summons supported by an affidavit, and the affidavit must be filed simultaneously with the summons.

The procedural requirements for setting aside enforcement orders serve the objectives of the Arbitration Ordinance by preventing uncertainty about the status of an award and ensuring parties are not left to speculate about the grounds for an application.

candidates.case_name: SKY POWER CONSTRUCTION ENGINEERING LTD v. IRAERO AIRLINES JSC
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Abuse of process in setting aside enforcement orders
principles.paraphrase_en: It constitutes an abuse of process to issue a summons to set aside an order granting leave to enforce an arbitral award if the summons fails to clearly disclose a valid ground for setting aside or is not supported by a proper affidavit as required by the Rules of the High Court.

principles.principle_label: Discretion to extend time under Order 3 rule 5
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's discretion to extend time under Order 3 rule 5 of the Rules of the High Court is broad and unrestricted, aimed at doing justice between the parties by balancing the principle of promoting the enforcement of arbitral awards with the principle of ensuring a party is not unfairly denied the opportunity to have its case heard.

principles.principle_label: Factors for extending time to set aside
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering an extension of time to apply to set aside an enforcement order, relevant factors include the length and reasons for the delay, whether the applicant acted reasonably, any contribution to the delay by the respondent, potential prejudice to the respondent, the strength of the setting aside application, impact on the arbitration, and overall fairness.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of merits in extension applications
principles.paraphrase_en: The merits of the underlying application to set aside an enforcement order are a relevant factor when deciding whether to grant an extension of time, as the court's primary aim is to secure the just resolution of disputes in accordance with the parties' substantive rights.

An arbitral tribunal has wide discretion, under the arbitration rules and the Arbitration Ordinance, to determine procedural and evidential matters, including the form and conduct of hearings, provided it acts fairly and avoids unnecessary delay or expense.

relationships.target_case_name: KB v S
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle established in KB v S that issuing a summons to set aside an enforcement order without a clear ground or proper affidavit is an abuse of process.

relationships.target_case_name: Astro Nusantara International BV v PT Ayunda Prima Mitra
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Final Appeal's test from Astro, which outlines the broad and unrestricted discretion of the court when extending time under Order 3 rule 5 RHC.

relationships.target_case_name: Terna Bahrain Holding Co WLL v Al Shamsi
relationships.description: The judgment cites Terna Bahrain for the list of relevant factors to consider when deciding whether to grant an extension of time to set aside an enforcement order.

candidates.case_name: KEIRON PETER ARCH ROCHESTER v. GLORY SKY PRECIOUS METALS LTD
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Reciprocal enforcement not conclusive for security for costs
principles.paraphrase_en: The existence of reciprocal enforcement arrangements between jurisdictions is not, by itself, a decisive factor against ordering security for costs under Order 23, rule 1(1)(a) of the Rules of the High Court. However, in the absence of other relevant evidence, it would generally be unjust to make such an order solely based on this factor.

relationships.target_case_name: Lim Yi Shenn v Wong Yuen Yee
relationships.description: The present judgment cites and quotes the reasoning of Recorder Horace Wong SC from this case regarding the treatment of reciprocal enforcement in security for costs applications.

candidates.case_name: S v. K
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Procedure for setting aside enforcement order
principles.paraphrase_en: An application to set aside an Enforcement Order must be made by summons supported by an affidavit, with both documents filed simultaneously. The summons and affidavit must clearly state the grounds and evidence relied upon for setting aside the order.

principles.principle_label: Extension of time for setting aside application
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may extend the time to apply to set aside an Enforcement Order if compelling reasons exist. The applicant must provide good reasons for the delay and demonstrate that the underlying application has merit.

principles.principle_label: Abuse of process in overlapping applications
principles.paraphrase_en: Filing a new summons on the same substantive issue while an existing application for an extension of time to deal with that issue is pending and under court directions may constitute an abuse of process.

principles.principle_label: Approach to granting extension of time
principles.paraphrase_en: When deciding whether to grant an extension of time, the court must consider all relevant matters holistically to achieve overall justice, rather than rigidly prioritizing certain factors over others.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for arbitrator bias
principles.paraphrase_en: A party alleging arbitrator bias bears the burden of proof. Mere misunderstanding of the procedure or the fact that arbitration hearings are held at the opposing party's solicitor's office is insufficient, without more, to establish bias or valid grounds to oppose enforcement.

relationships.target_case_name: K&C Asia Pacific Ltd v G
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the requirement to clearly state grounds and evidence in an application to set aside an Enforcement Order.

relationships.target_case_name: KB v S
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from this case regarding the requirements for extending time to apply to set aside an Enforcement Order.

relationships.target_case_name: Astro Nusantara International BV v BT Ayunda Prima Mitra
relationships.description: The judgment applies the holistic approach from this case for considering all relevant factors when deciding on an extension of time.

candidates.case_name: 深圳市深超科技投資有限公司 v. 世紀晶源科技有限公司 AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Kwan, Chu VPP and Yuen JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Prohibition of double enforcement and recovery
principles.paraphrase_en: Section 93 of the Arbitration Ordinance (Cap. 609) prohibits both double enforcement and double recovery of an arbitral award. If an award holder has applied for enforcement in the Mainland, they must wait until those proceedings are concluded and the amount recovered is ascertained before applying for enforcement in Hong Kong.

Section 93 of the Arbitration Ordinance goes to the jurisdiction of the Hong Kong court to enforce an award. If its conditions are not met, the court has no jurisdiction to grant an enforcement order.
The exception under section 93(2) of the Arbitration Ordinance, allowing enforcement for the unsatisfied part of an award, applies only if the award 'is not fully satisfied'.

principles.principle_label: No appeal from setting aside of enforcement leave
principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Appeal has no jurisdiction to grant leave to appeal a decision setting aside leave for enforcement of an arbitral award. The unsuccessful party must instead resort to the residual jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal to set aside the refusal of leave decision.

principles.principle_label: High threshold for challenging refusal of leave
principles.paraphrase_en: A party challenging a judge's refusal of leave to appeal a decision has an 'extraordinarily high hurdle to surmount'. The residual jurisdiction to set aside such a refusal is only engaged in exceptional circumstances, such as where the judge reached a conclusion not through an intellectual process but through bias, whim, or irrelevant factors.

relationships.target_case_name: Shenzhen Kai Loong Investment and Development Co Ltd v CEC Electrical Manufacturing (International) Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment follows its reasoning that both double enforcement and double recovery are prohibited under the relevant ordinance.

relationships.target_case_name: Guangdong Changhong Electric Co Ltd v Inspurs Electronics (HK) Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies its holding that the Court of Appeal has no jurisdiction to grant leave to appeal a decision setting aside enforcement leave.

relationships.target_case_name: China International Fund Ltd v Dennis Lau & Ng Chun Man Architects & Engineers (HK) Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies its holding regarding the lack of jurisdiction to grant leave to appeal and the need to resort to residual jurisdiction.

relationships.target_case_name: Kyla Shipping Co Ltd v Bunge SA
relationships.description: The judgment cites it for the principle that challenging a refusal of leave presents an 'extraordinarily high hurdle'.

relationships.target_case_name: Aden Refinery Co Ltd v Ugland Management Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites Mustill LJ's dictum on when the residual jurisdiction to set aside a refusal of leave might be engaged.

relationships.target_case_name: Cetelem SA v Roust Holdings Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment finds this English authority does not assist the applicant, as it concerned a different statutory context regarding jurisdictional decisions.

candidates.case_name: A CONSORTIUM COMPRISING TPL AND ICB v. AE LTD
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Hong Kong courts generally adopt a mechanistic approach to enforcing arbitral awards under the Arbitration Ordinance, meaning the court's role is limited to entering judgment exactly as stated in the award.
Despite the mechanistic approach, courts retain flexibility to avoid rigid application if it would contradict the Ordinance's purpose of facilitating arbitration agreement and award enforcement.
The Arbitration Ordinance does not prevent a court from enforcing only part of an arbitral award where appropriate.

relationships.target_case_name: Xiamen v Eaton Properties Limited and ors
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the proposition that courts have flexibility to enforce part of an award and are not bound to a rigid mechanistic approach.

candidates.case_name: SIC v. WI AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: To resist enforcement of an arbitral award on the basis of fraud, the resisting party must demonstrate a real prospect of success in proving the award was obtained by fraud, which is a high burden.

When deciding whether to order security for the adjournment of enforcement proceedings, the court should compare the creditor's position if allowed to enforce immediately against its position if enforcement is delayed.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of award size in enforcement difficulty
principles.paraphrase_en: The substantial size of an arbitral award, including the principal sum and accrued interest, is a relevant factor in assessing the difficulty of enforcement if proceedings are adjourned.

relationships.target_case_name: Karaha Bodas Co LLP v Perusahaan Pertambangan Minydak Dan Gas Bumi Negara
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that resisting enforcement for fraud requires showing a 'real prospect of success'.

relationships.target_case_name: Mayer Corporation Development Ltd v Alliance Financial Intelligence Ltd and others
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the high burden of establishing that an award was obtained by fraud.

relationships.target_case_name: A v B
relationships.description: The judgment applies the guideline from this case on comparing the creditor's position with and without delayed enforcement when considering security.

relationships.target_case_name: 中國機床銷售與技術服務有限公司 v 國晟機電設備有限公司
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from this case that the size of an award is relevant to demonstrating enforcement difficulty.

candidates.case_name: 湖州升華金融服務有限公司 v. HANG PIN LIVING TECHNOLOGY COMPANY LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Phoebe Man in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Validity of choice of court agreement
principles.paraphrase_en: The validity of a choice of Mainland court agreement is separate from the validity of other contractual terms, including guarantee provisions, under the relevant ordinance.
A registration order for a Mainland judgment must be set aside if it fails to comply with the specific requirements set out in section 5(2)(e) of the relevant ordinance.

relationships.target_case_name: Mainland Judgment
relationships.description: The court distinguishes the Mainland Judgment's finding on the guarantee's invalidity, holding it does not affect the separate validity of the choice of court clause.

relationships.target_case_name: Appeal Judgment
relationships.description: The court applies the Appeal Judgment's characterization of the case as involving a valid main contract with an invalid guarantee.

candidates.case_name: DENG MIAN v. PAN RONG
judges: Before: Hon H. Au-Yeung J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Right to hearing and oral submissions
principles.paraphrase_en: The right to a fair hearing does not invariably require oral submissions, particularly at first instance where no factual findings are necessary and the court deems oral argument unnecessary; the court may properly decide the matter based on written submissions alone.

principles.principle_label: Enforceability of jurisdiction clauses in void agreements
principles.paraphrase_en: An argument that a jurisdiction clause within a loan agreement is rendered ineffective because the underlying loan agreement itself has been held to be void is not accepted as a valid basis to challenge the clause's enforceability or its legal consequences.

principles.principle_label: Excluded judgment under Reciprocal Enforcement Ordinance
principles.paraphrase_en: A judgment from a Mainland court is an 'excluded judgment' that cannot be registered for enforcement in Hong Kong if it was given pursuant to a choice of Mainland court agreement that was made before the commencement date of the New Reciprocal Enforcement Ordinance.

relationships.target_case_name: Chow Shun Yung v Wei Pih & Another
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Chow Shun Yung that the right to a hearing does not always mandate oral submissions.

candidates.case_name: S v. G
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The court has discretion to stay enforcement of an award or judgment, and must consider all relevant circumstances, including potential prejudice to either the award creditor (if denied immediate enforcement) or the debtor (if forced to pay pending resolution of a cross-claim.

For a counterclaim to operate as an equitable set-off against a debt, there must be a sufficiently close connection between the transaction giving rise to the plaintiff's claim and the defendant's counterclaim.

When considering a stay of enforcement based on a cross-claim, the court may consider the strength and merits of that cross-claim, including whether liability has already been determined in a prior proceeding, leaving only quantification of damages.

relationships.target_case_name: Esso Petroleum Co Ltd v Milton
relationships.description: The judgment references Esso Petroleum to illustrate the principle that a counterclaim requires a close connection to the main claim to qualify as an equitable set-off.

relationships.target_case_name: Hanak v Green
relationships.description: The judgment notes that Esso Petroleum referenced Hanak v Green for its description of the required 'close relationship' for equitable set-off.

candidates.case_name: SONG LIHUA v. LEE CHEE HON (FORMER NAME: QUE WENBIN)
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Hong Kong courts adopt a policy favoring arbitration and enforcement, seeking to uphold arbitration agreements and enforce awards, intervening only as expressly permitted by the Arbitration Ordinance.

principles.principle_label: No obligation to set aside award before opposing enforcement
principles.paraphrase_en: A party opposing enforcement of an arbitral award in Hong Kong is not required to first apply to set aside the award in the jurisdiction where it was made.

principles.principle_label: Public policy ground for refusing enforcement
principles.paraphrase_en: Refusing enforcement of an arbitral award on public policy grounds requires a finding that the award is contrary to the forum's fundamental conceptions of morality and justice, involving a substantial injustice so shocking as to make enforcement repugnant.

principles.principle_label: Audi alteram partem as fundamental natural justice
principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of audi alteram partem (the right to a fair hearing where each party can respond to evidence and present its case) is a fundamental principle of natural justice enforced in Hong Kong.

principles.principle_label: Requirement of tribunal alertness for fair hearing
principles.paraphrase_en: A hearing must be conducted by a tribunal whose members are concentrating on the case; a hearing may be unfair if a member appears not to be alert.

relationships.target_case_name: Paklito Investment Limited v Klockner East Asia Limited
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Paklito that a defendant is not obliged to seek setting aside of an award in its home jurisdiction before opposing enforcement in Hong Kong.

relationships.target_case_name: Hebei Import & Export Corp
relationships.description: The judgment follows the Court of Final Appeal's definition of 'contrary to public policy' as meaning contrary to the fundamental conceptions of morality and justice of the forum.

relationships.target_case_name: A v R (Arbitration: Enforcement)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the test from A v R that the public policy ground requires a substantial injustice so shocking to the conscience as to render enforcement repugnant.

relationships.target_case_name: Stansbury v Datapulse plc and anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites Stansbury for the principle that a tribunal member's lack of alertness during a hearing may render the hearing unfair.

judges: Before: Mr Recorder Manzoni SC in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Indirect enforcement of foreign revenue laws
principles.paraphrase_en: Hong Kong courts will not enforce foreign penal or revenue laws through civil proceedings, as this constitutes an assertion of sovereign authority by one state within the territory of another.

principles.principle_label: Essential feature of the revenue rule
principles.paraphrase_en: A necessary condition for the rule against enforcing foreign revenue laws to apply is the existence of an unsatisfied tax claim to which the proceeds of the action would be applied.
If the foreign tax has already been paid and there is no unsatisfied tax claim, the rule against indirect enforcement of foreign revenue laws does not apply to prevent enforcement of a judgment.

relationships.target_case_name: Government of India v Taylor
relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Keith's explanation that foreign tax claims are an extension of sovereign power and an assertion of authority within another state's territory.

relationships.target_case_name: Williams & Humbert
relationships.description: The judgment follows the House of Lords' reasoning that an unsatisfied claim to which proceeds will be applied is an essential feature of the principle against enforcing foreign revenue laws.

relationships.target_case_name: HMRC v Shahdadpuri
relationships.description: The judgment references this case where the court distinguished enforcement of revenue laws from actions related to fraud that effectively stole money from the revenue authority.

relationships.target_case_name: Cido Car Carrier Service Limited v Woori Bank (Hong Kong Branch)
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case involving South Korean tax authorities freezing funds in Hong Kong to satisfy a tax liability.
